docs.title: Gameplay
docs.text: This section covers core systems that shape how you play: missions, technology, skills, activities (PvP, PvE, Arena, Invasions), and events.

docs.title: Core Systems
docs.text:
• Missions -- Personal, corp, and alliance missions.
  • Combat and Targeting -- Target locks, attack readiness, hostile ability gates, and defense attack rules.
  • Technology -- 250+ techs from artifacts, corps, and items.
  • Technology Reference -- Technology effect values.
  • Skills -- Captain, ship, and corporation skill trees (they stack).
  • Level and XP Reference -- Level formula and XP milestones.
  • Achievements and Leaderboards -- Tracked achievement families, leaderboard categories, and known milestone patterns.

docs.title: Activities
docs.text:
• Player vs Player (PvP) -- Open-world PvP and territory.
  • The Arena -- Structured PvP and Skull rewards.
  • Player vs Environment (PvE) -- PvE content.
  • Invasions -- Invasion events.

docs.title: Abilities
docs.text: • Abilities -- Grapple, Hack, Drain, Technician, Taunt, Deflector, Junker, Small Strike Team Bonus.

docs.title: Events
docs.text: • Events -- Free daily gifts, daily modifiers, holiday gems, Party Boxes, Reward Point Gifts.

docs.title: Other
docs.text:
• Oblivion Server -- Separate hardcore universe; different PvP, durability, and economy rules.
  • Spaceball -- In-game game of chance (Credits/Tech Points); availability may vary by region.

docs.title: Achievements and Leaderboards
docs.text:
Achievements and leaderboards track long-term progress across combat, missions, crafting, loot, events, PvP, and corporation activity. This page is a reference for what is tracked, not a strategy guide or a complete reward catalog.

Exact milestone lists, rewards, and title unlocks still need more verification before they should be published as complete tables.

docs.title: Use This For
docs.text: Need Use Checking whether an activity is tracked Use the leaderboard and achievement family tables below. Finding related rules Follow the links to the activity, item, PvP, or structure page. Comparing long-term goals Use the milestone pattern notes, but treat incomplete ladders as Needs data. Strategy or efficient routing Use strategy pages instead of this reference page.

docs.title: Leaderboard Categories
docs.text:
Category Tracked areas Player Achievements, major achievements, critical achievements, titles, ship XP, defense XP, resources harvested, credits earned, Spaceball credits, Spaceball Tech Points, and Party Boxes. Arena Arena wins, Hardcore Arena wins, arena kills, Hardcore arena kills, Arena Phoenix, and Arena Gladiator. Missions Solo missions, corporation missions, alliance missions, universe missions, and mission Tech Points. Crafting Ultimate ships, Interdictor, CPU kits, items, blueprints, ships, Assassin, Hades, Carrier, and Emperor. Loot Items looted, Legendary loot, Ultimate loot, relics, and fragments harvested. PvE NPC kills, NPC planet/garrison/outpost/shipyard kills, Pirate kills, Precursor kills, Wyrd kills, Het kills, major leader kills, minor leader kills, and Assassin leader kills. Invasions Invasion wins, invasion items looted, invasion leaders, and invasion bosses. PvP Players killed, Hardcore players killed, Skulls earned, and PvP planet/garrison/outpost/shipyard kills.

Many leaderboard categories may also have corporation-facing versions, but each category still needs to be checked before the wiki claims a corporation leaderboard exists.

docs.title: Achievement Families
docs.text: Family Tracked examples Arena Arena wins and Hardcore Arena wins. Events Party Boxes opened, Party Boxes completed, and Rift Daily Challenge completion. Missions Solo, corporation, alliance, and universe mission completion. Corporation contribution Corporation XP contribution and corporation Tech Point contribution. Leader kills Major leaders, minor leaders, and Assassin leaders. Ships Deploying, scrapping, and building ships by faction and ship class. Items Scrapping, looting, repairing, upgrading, and deconstructing items by item category and rarity. PvP Player kills, Hardcore player kills, Skulls earned, and PvP structure kills. Structures Planet, Garrison, Outpost, and Shipyard kills; faction structure kills for Wyrd, Pirate, Het, and Precursor; structure repair and level-up achievements.

docs.title: Known Milestone Patterns
docs.text: Activity Known pattern Arena wins Dense early milestones from 1 through 20 wins, then larger milestones up to 5,000. Party Boxes opened Known milestones include 1, 5, 10, 25, 50, and 100 opened. Party Boxes completed Known milestones include 1, then 5 through 100 in larger steps, with a longer ladder through 1,000 completed. Rift Daily Challenge Long-tail milestones continue through 5,000 completions. Corporation XP contribution Known milestones include 10, 50, 100, 250, 500, 750; 1,000 through 10,000 in 500-step milestones; 20,000, 30,000, 40,000, 50,000; 100,000 through 500,000 in 100,000-step milestones; and 1,000,000. Leader kills Known ladders begin with 1, 5, 10, 25, 50, and 100 kills, then continue into larger long-term milestones.

docs.title: Item Achievement Axes
docs.text:
Item achievements are tracked across these item categories:

Category Related page Weapon Weapons Armor Equipment Slots Storage Equipment Slots Drone Drones Engine Equipment Slots Computer Tech Computers Special Special Items Shield Equipment Slots Battery Equipment Slots

Item rarity achievements use the public rarity names: Common, Uncommon, Rare, Ultra-Rare, Elite, Legendary, and Ultimate.

Known repair and upgrade achievement families appear to begin at Uncommon. The exact public milestone tables still need verification.

docs.text:

  • Exact rewards, titles, and unlocks from each achievement family.
  • Which leaderboard categories have corporation versions.
  • Current UI labels and in-game paths for each leaderboard view.
  • Whether Skull Pile crafting achievements are live, event-limited, or legacy.

docs.title: Combat and Targeting
docs.text: Combat actions are built around target rules, target locks, item readiness, ability gates, and protection checks. This page covers the shared rules; item-specific details stay on Weapons, Drones, and Abilities.

docs.title: Target Locks
docs.text:
Before weapons or Fighter drones can fire, the ship must acquire a target lock.

Factor Effect Size difference Large ships take longer to lock small targets. Targeting Speed Reduces lock time for weapons and drones. Interdictor penalties Can increase movement and targeting time in affected sectors. Minimum lock time Lock time cannot go below the game's minimum lock-time floor.

Defense units ignore most size-based targeting limits and target nearly instantly.

docs.title: Size and Targeting
docs.text:
Every combat entity has a size: Small, Medium, Large, Huge, or Massive. Size affects movement, evasion, and targeting.

Small ships are harder for large ships to lock. Fighter drones ignore size disparity, which makes them useful for large ships that need to hit small targets.

For size tables, movement warm-up, and minimum move times, see Size and Targeting.

docs.title: Attack Requirements
docs.text:
A ship needs at least one Weapon or Fighter Drone to make normal attacks.

Targets with a non-enemy relation cannot be attacked. Protected targets cannot be attacked unless the attacker has a valid protection bypass state.

docs.title: Attack Readiness
docs.text:
Weapons and Fighter drones both use target-lock timing and cooldown timing.

When choosing the next attack item, the game compares each ready weapon or Fighter drone and uses the candidate with the lowest remaining effective wait. Disabled weapon slots are ignored. Clearing a target resets weapon and drone lock timers that have not already locked.

For drone-specific timing, see Drones. For weapon-slot and item behavior, see Weapons.

docs.title: Hostile Abilities
docs.text:
Some abilities are hostile actions and require a target in the same sector.

Ability Target required Hostile Battery Grapple Yes Yes Costs battery Hack Yes Yes Costs battery Drain Yes Yes No battery cost Strike Varies by source Yes No battery cost Purge Yes No No battery cost Repair Yes No No battery cost

Grapple, Hack, and Drain require 10+ of the matching stat to use and have a default 50 s cooldown. See Abilities for ability-specific effects and technology lines.

docs.title: Ability Gates
docs.text:
Common ability checks include:

  • The ship must be allowed to use the ability in its current context.
  • The ability must not be on cooldown.
  • The ship must have enough battery for abilities with a battery cost.
  • Target-required abilities require a target in the same sector.
  • Target-required abilities cannot target destroyed entities.
  • Hostile abilities require a hostile target and respect protection rules.
  • Repair requires the target to be below maximum hull.
  • Purge requires the target to have a removable negative status.

If a ship is hacked, hackable abilities may show the hacked timer instead of the normal cooldown timer.

docs.title: Defense Unit Attacks
docs.text:
Defense units can only be attacked by players in an alliance. Defense-unit attacks also require the attacking ship to meet the required Gear Score and respect any attack-protection timer.

For structure warfare rewards and PvP context, see Structure Warfare Rules and Skulls.

docs.title: Arena Restrictions
docs.text:
Arena rules can block certain actions or gear states:

  • The ship must have at least one Weapon or Fighter Drone.
  • Free starter items cannot be used.
  • Strike is not usable in Arena.
  • Some Arena modes or daily modifiers add extra restrictions.

See Arena for the full Arena rule page.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Weapons
  • Drones
  • Abilities
  • Size and Targeting
  • Interdictor Carrier
  • Player vs Player

docs.title: Level and XP Reference
docs.text:
Level is calculated from total XP. Level is capped at 500; XP at or above 235,261,135 returns level 500.0.

XP below 540 returns integer level 1.

docs.title: Fractional Level Formula
docs.text:
Between level thresholds, level progress uses:

level = current integer level + (current XP - XP required for current level) / (XP required for next level - XP required for current level)

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Skills
  • Technology Reference
  • Ships

docs.title: Missions
docs.text: Each captain can have up to three captain missions at a time. Mission categories include Tutorial Missions, system-chain missions, shared Corp Mission, shared Alliance Missions, and one server-wide Universe Mission.

docs.title: Mission Limits & Deletion
docs.text:
• You can get up to 4 new personal missions per day, whether by completing missions or deleting them to receive replacements.
  • Corp missions are progressed by all corp members.
  • Alliance missions are progressed by all alliance members.
  • Universe missions are progressed by all players on the server.
  • You must contribute at least 1 progress to a shared mission to get its reward.
  • Leaving your corp resets your shared mission participation (you lose progress toward corp/alliance mission rewards).

Corporations can cancel their corp or alliance missions using the :CORP and :ALLIANCE chat commands.

docs.title: Mission Rewards and Refresh
docs.text:
Observed base mission rewards:

Completion Base reward Personal, shared, or universal mission 250 XP and 5 Tech Points During Mission rewards doubled daily event 500 XP and 10 Tech Points

Additional shared-mission rules:

  • When a corporation, alliance, or universe mission completes, any player who contributed at least 1 progress gets the full player reward.
  • When a corporation or alliance mission completes, any corporation with a contributing player gets double the base reward to the corporation.
  • Corp and universe missions refresh once per day. Completing more than one in the same day does not guarantee another new one until the next day.
  • Alliance mission availability depends on alliance corporations; each corporation in an alliance can have its own alliance mission once per day.
  • A non-personal mission with no progress for 3 days can be automatically cancelled.

Needs confirmation

Mission reward values should be checked in game after mission-system patches.

docs.title: Mission Types
docs.text: Type Description Kill Normal NPCs Standard enemy kills. Kill Elite NPCs Elite NPCs progress by 1 point per Elite Rank (e.g. a 3-Star Elite = 3 points). Kill NPC Leaders Leader kills. Kill NPC Defense Units Destroy NPC structures. Kill Players Only kills outside The Arena count, and only when a Skull is earned. 1 point per kill per mission max. Harvest Resources Gather from asteroids and nodes. Explore Solar Systems Visit and explore systems on the Starmap. Deposit Resources Deposit resources. Craft Item Craft an item. Upgrade Item Upgrade an item. Equip Item Equip an item. Scrap Item Scrap an item. Mine Mine an asteroid. Rescue Rescue crew. Distress Answer a distress signal. Outpost Deposit Deposit a specified resource to an outpost. Hunt Named NPC Kill a named leader.

docs.title: Mission overlap rules
docs.text:
Mission overlap rules:

  • Multiple personal missions can progress from the same action. For example, one NPC kill can progress two compatible personal kill missions.
  • Multiple overlapping alliance missions do not all progress from the same action; the most recently acquired matching mission receives progress.
  • Only one corporation mission and one universe mission can be active at a time.

docs.title: Important Rules
docs.text:
• "Aliens" in missions -- Means any NPC enemy, including Pirates. Mission text that says "Aliens" can include Pirates unless specified otherwise.
  • Corporate Defenders -- Corporate Defender kills do not count for mission kill credit.
  • Outpost missions -- Some missions are tied to a specific outpost. Use the mission question-mark details to identify the source. See Outpost Missions.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Corporations -- Corp and alliance missions
  • PvE -- Many mission objectives are PvE
  • Outpost Missions -- Outpost-specific chains and turn-ins
  • Mission Strategy -- Community mission planning notes
  • PvP -- Player kill missions
  • Skulls -- Earned from PvP kills that count for missions

docs.title: Oblivion Server
docs.text:
The Oblivion Server is a separate universe with hardcore rule variations. Access: Login Screen -> Select Character -> Oblivion Mode -> Create Character.

No refunds

Staff will never refund or repair items or ships on the Oblivion Server -- even for deaths caused by lag, bugs, or staff error. You agree to this by playing there.

docs.title: Differences from the Primary Server
docs.text: Rule Oblivion Primary Loot / XP / Crafting Accelerated Normal PvP Everywhere except systems near Earth Only in non-human systems Ships on Jump Gates Can be attacked in PvP maps Relatively safe when not in combat Corporation size Max 15 members Larger Alliance size Max 2 corporations Up to 6 Item repair Cannot repair items Can repair Items to Ultimate Cannot upgrade with Tech Points Can upgrade Ship durability on death 10% loss; ship breaks at 0% Small loss; repairable with Skulls Ship drops on death 1 random equipped item No drop (in open world) Hardcore ship on death 100% durability loss; all equipped items drop 1 random item drop Hardcore ship loot Large loot bonus (see below) -- Following Captains can only follow their own ships Can follow allies Love Day No Love Day mechanics Love Day disables PvP Ship movement Faster Normal Crafters Can create Legendary and Ultimate items Restricted Free ships / relic level-up No free ships or relic level-up rewards Yes Defense unit Gear Score 2,000 required (recall if lower) 3,000 SR8 capture 7 Alliance Citizens required 20 Skull cost Reduced for building or Elite Rank ships Normal Unique ships 1% durability loss on death --

docs.title: Hardcore Ships on Oblivion
docs.text: Hardcore ships generate pity loot at 2x the Oblivion accelerated rate when they get the killing shot. This stacks with other bonuses -- up to 10x or more during invasions.

docs.title: Arena on Oblivion
docs.text:
• Hardcore ships -- Drop one item on death (not everything).
  • Normal ships -- Do not drop any equipment on death.
  • Equipment -- Takes 1% durability damage on death.
  • Ship durability -- 1% loss on death.
  • Rewards -- The Arena can award Ultimate items.

docs.title: Tech Points and Reward Points
docs.text:
• Tech Point orders deliver to both Primary and Oblivion if you have a character on each. There is no link between servers; nothing transfers.
  • Reward Point Gifts can buy Party Boxes that include Ultimate rarity on Oblivion.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• PvP -- Normal server rules
  • Ships -- Hardcore ships
  • Tech Points
  • Factions -- The Precursors' conflict with The Oblivion (lore)

docs.title: Skills
docs.text:
Captains, Ships, Corporations, and defense structures each have a Skill Tree. Points invested give permanent stat improvements -- and all of these stack (captain + corp + ship).

New skill points come from leveling and from studying Artifacts. Captains study artifacts captured at corporation planets and garrisons.

Skills can be invested up to 10 points per stat. The bonus is reduced above 5 points.

Hardcore ships and defense units gain bonus skill points and can invest an additional 5 max points per stat.

Like stats from items and technology, skill bonuses are subject to diminishing returns.

docs.title: New-player guidance
docs.text: Early skill recommendations belong in Getting Started or Strategy pages, not in this rules page.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Stats
  • Diminishing Returns
  • New Player Path

docs.title: Spaceball
docs.text:
Availability

Spaceball may not be available in some countries or on some app platforms.

Spaceball is a simulated game of chance that uses in-game currency (Credits or Tech Points), not real money. It is simple to learn and easy to play.

Open CHAT and select the SPACEBALL tab to begin.

docs.title: Basics
docs.text:
• Table -- There are 38 numbers: 1 through 36, plus 0 and 00.
  • Colors -- Numbers 1-36 alternate RED or BLACK. 0 and 00 are GREEN.
  • Roll -- The Spaceball rolls every minute to select a number.
  • Bets -- You can place bets any time before the roll. Bets continue even if you close the chat window.

docs.title: Placing Bets
docs.text:
1. Click a number or betting field on the table.
  2. Choose how many Credits or Tech Points to bet.
  3. Winning bets -- Payout is given immediately on the roll; your bet is returned with the payout.
  4. Losing bets -- The bet is removed from the table.
  5. You can click a bet at any time to take it back.

Repeat Bet -- In the bet dialog you can set a "Repeat Bet" count. If the bet loses, the same bet is re-placed automatically (even if you close the chat window).

docs.title: Bet Types
docs.text:
• Straight-Up Bet -- Bet on a single number. Best payout (35 to 1) but riskiest.
  • Dozen bets -- 1st Dozen (1-12), 2nd Dozen (13-24), 3rd Dozen (25-36): 2 to 1.
  • RED or BLACK -- 1 to 1. Good for beginners.
  • EVEN or ODD -- 1 to 1.
  • LOW (1-18) or HIGH (19-36) -- 1 to 1.

RED/BLACK and EVEN/ODD are simpler bet types for beginners.

docs.title: Payouts
docs.text: Bet Payout Straight-Up (single number) 35 to 1 1st Dozen (1-12) 2 to 1 2nd Dozen (13-24) 2 to 1 3rd Dozen (25-36) 2 to 1 RED or BLACK 1 to 1 EVEN or ODD 1 to 1 1 to 18 (LOW) 1 to 1 19 to 36 (HIGH) 1 to 1

docs.title: Buttons
docs.text:
• STATS -- Roll history, streaks, and other data.
  • MY LOG -- Your personal bets and wins.
  • CONFIG -- Mute chat or sounds.

docs.title: Responsible Play
docs.text:
Only gamble what you don't mind losing.

Games are supposed to be fun. Anyone can develop a gambling problem. If you find yourself upset or not having fun, stop playing. Nobody ever lost by walking away.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Currencies -- Credits and Tech Points
  • Tech Points -- Can be used for Spaceball or upgrades

docs.title: Technology Reference
docs.text: This page collects technology effect tables. Use Technology for the overview and source rules.

docs.title: Use This For
docs.text: If you need to know... Use Move speed, targeting, Space Lane, or Interdictor values Movement and Targeting XP, Evasion, or Hit Chance bonuses XP, Evasion, and Hit Chance Hull and shield maximum bonuses Hull and Shield Maximums Outgoing or incoming damage modifiers Damage Modifiers Nearby repair facility bonuses Repair Facility

docs.title: Movement and Targeting
docs.text:
Negative move-time or targeting-time modifiers are faster. Positive Interdictor values are penalties.

Tech/effect Effect HyperDrive1 -10% move time modifier HyperDrive2 -15% move time modifier HyperDrive3 -20% move time modifier SpaceLaneEffect1 -10% move time modifier SpaceLaneEffect2 -15% move time modifier SpaceLaneEffect3 -20% move time modifier FriendlyTerritoryEffect -10% move time modifier NanobotCharge -10% move time modifier Targeting1 -5% targeting-time modifier Targeting2 -10% targeting-time modifier Targeting3 -15% targeting-time modifier InterdictorEffect1 +15% move and targeting-time penalty InterdictorEffect2 +20% move and targeting-time penalty InterdictorEffect3 +25% move and targeting-time penalty InterdictorEffect4 +30% move and targeting-time penalty

docs.title: XP, Evasion, and Hit Chance
docs.text: Tech Effect Analysis1 +10% XP Analysis2 +15% XP Analysis3 +20% XP Intuition1 +5% XP and +5 evasion Intuition2 +10% XP and +10 evasion Intuition3 +15% XP and +15 evasion EvasiveManeuvers1 +6 evasion EvasiveManeuvers2 +10 evasion EvasiveManeuvers3 +14 evasion Intimidation1 +6 hit chance Intimidation2 +10 hit chance Intimidation3 +14 hit chance

docs.title: Hull and Shield Maximums
docs.text:
For highest-rank-only rows, use only the strongest active rank from that family.

Tech family Effect HullMax1-8 Highest rank only: +5%, +8%, +11%, +14%, +17%, +20%, +23%, +30% hull AdvancedAlloys1-2 Additional +2%, +4% hull ShieldMax1-7 Highest rank only: +5%, +8%, +11%, +14%, +17%, +20%, +23% shield ShieldAmplifier1-3 Additional +3%, +6%, +9% shield

docs.title: Damage Modifiers
docs.text:
Outgoing damage increases damage dealt. Incoming damage increases damage taken.

Tech/effect Effect Overpower +10% outgoing damage and +15% incoming damage Vanquisher +15% outgoing damage SmallStrikeTeamEffect +5% outgoing damage NanobotCharge +10% outgoing damage LeadershipEffect1 +3% outgoing damage LeadershipEffect2 +5% outgoing damage LeadershipEffect3 +7% outgoing damage Freighter1 -50% outgoing damage Freighter2 -45% outgoing damage Freighter3 -40% outgoing damage Freighter4 -35% outgoing damage Freighter5 -30% outgoing damage EnemyTerritoryEffect +10% incoming damage

docs.title: Repair Facility
docs.text: Tech Hull-repair bonus RepairFacility1 +10% RepairFacility2 +15% RepairFacility3 +20% RepairFacility4 +25%

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Technology
  • Skills
  • Artifacts
  • Ship Mods

docs.title: Technology
docs.text:
There are over 250 technologies in TIB2 that affect combat, movement, crafting, and more. They come from Artifacts, corporation research, special items, and alien ships.

  • Ranked tech (e.g. Improved Hulls) does not stack with lower ranks.
  • Non-ranked tech (e.g. Analysis, Targeting) stacks with other similar benefits.

Some low-level technology can be boosted for one hour using Tech Points. The timer does not count down while that technology is superseded by a better or similar technology, such as corporation or artifact technology.

docs.title: Common sources
docs.text: Source Notes Artifacts Give corporation technology bonuses while controlled. Corporations Corporation research and technology can benefit member ships. Items Some item types and special items unlock technologies or technology-like effects. Alien ships Rare alien ships can provide access to stronger or different technology. Tech Points Can temporarily boost some low-level technologies.

docs.title: Stacking and item slots
docs.text:
Technology from items applies at full strength even when the item is in an auxiliary equipment slot. The 10% auxiliary-slot rule affects item stats, not the technology attached to the item.

Corporation technology, artifact technology, item technology, and ship/faction technology can all contribute to a build, subject to the ranked/non-ranked stacking rules above.

docs.title: Full list
docs.text: Use the in-game HELP -> INFORMATION -> TECHNOLOGY LIST menu for the complete technology list.

docs.title: Contributor notes
docs.text: Do not duplicate every technology on related pages. Put broad technology rules here, then link to dedicated pages for specific mechanics such as Technician, Deflector, Junker, Taunt, Grapple, Hacking, and Drain.

docs.title: Abilities
docs.text:
Hotbar abilities and special technologies that shape combat: Grapple, Hack, Drain, Technician (repairs), Taunt (tank), Deflector, and Junker. Many hostile abilities use the same stat-vs-resist pattern: the matching Chance stat strengthens the effect, the matching Resist stat weakens it, and player Hit Chance/Evasion can contribute.

Shared target, cooldown, battery, and hostile-action gates live on Combat and Targeting.

docs.title: Hotbar Abilities
docs.text:
Grapple, Hack, and Drain require 10+ of the matching stat to use, require a target in the same sector, and have a default 50 s cooldown. Grapple and Hack cost battery; Drain does not.

Ability Stat Effect See Grapple Grapple Chance Target loses 25 Evasion and cannot move; costs battery. Grapple Hack Hack Chance Target can't use hotbar (except Repair), no battery regen, no special movement; costs battery. Hacking Drain Drain Chance Steals shields and battery; no battery cost to use. Drain

docs.title: Special Items & Tech
docs.text: Topic Source Effect Technician Technician Special item or Medic ship mod Repair upgrades: purge, critical/splash repair, repair-over-time (immune to Grapple/Hack/Taunt). Taunt Tank Special (Ultra-Rare+) Forces damaged enemies to attack the tank; Technician can remove. Deflector Deflector tech Chance to negate 100% damage and return it to attacker. Junker Junker tech Hull repair to attacker when they deal damage (33-100%; no Technician bonus).

docs.title: Fleet Bonus
docs.text: Topic Effect Small Strike Team Bonus Fleet of 4+ with >=33% Small or Medium: +5% Outgoing Damage and +5 all resists for the entire fleet.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Stats -- Grapple/Hack/Drain Chance and Resist
  • Combat and Targeting -- Shared ability gates and target rules
  • Ships -- What uses abilities
  • Special Items -- Special items (Technician, Tank, Deflector, Hacker, and more)

docs.title: Deflector Technology
docs.text:
Deflector gives a chance to negate all incoming damage and return it to the attacker. The negated amount is 100% (not random). The deflect roll happens after Hit/Evasion and Critical, in the final damage step. Deflector can trigger on any damage (weapons, drones, splash, battle ram).

Deflected damage is treated as an attack: it benefits from the defender's stats such as Outgoing Damage Bonus, Shield Pierce, etc.

Advanced Hacking III can disable a target's Special Item abilities, including Deflector.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Grapple | Hacking | Drain | Technician | Taunt | Junker
  • Abilities

docs.title: Drain Ability
docs.text: With 10+ Drain Chance, a ship can use the Drain hotbar action. Drain requires a target in the same sector, is a hostile action, has a default 50 s cooldown, and has no battery cost to activate. Drain steals some of the target's shields and battery, making weapons and other abilities harder or impossible to use.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
• Drain Chance increases the amount stolen.
  • Drain Resist reduces the amount stolen.
  • For player ships, Hit Chance can contribute to Drain power and Evasion can contribute to Drain resistance.
  • If the final effect is too weak, the Drain can be resisted.

Exact amount and resist formulas are Needs data until the live behavior is confirmed.

docs.title: Technology Effects
docs.text: Technology Effect Advanced Draining Adds +20 shield and +2 battery stolen by Drain. Advanced Draining II Adds +40 shield and +4 battery stolen by Drain. Advanced Draining III Adds +60 shield and +6 battery stolen by Drain and allows Drain to critical. Drain Speed Reduces Drain cooldown by 4 s. Drain Speed II Reduces Drain cooldown by 6 s. Drain Speed III Reduces Drain cooldown by 8 s. Reverse Drain Gives a high chance to deflect Drain back at attackers.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Grapple | Hacking | Technician | Taunt | Deflector | Junker
  • Stats -- Drain Chance / Resist
  • Abilities

docs.title: Grapple Ability
docs.text: With 10+ Grapple Chance, a ship can use the Grapple hotbar action. Grapple requires a target in the same sector, is a hostile action, has a default 50 s cooldown, and costs battery to use. Grappled targets lose 25 Evasion and cannot move while the effect lasts.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
• Grapple Chance increases the Grapple effect.
  • Grapple Resist reduces the Grapple effect.
  • For player ships, Hit Chance can contribute to Grapple power and Evasion can contribute to Grapple resistance.
  • If the final effect is too weak, the Grapple can be resisted.

Exact duration and resist formulas are Needs data until the live behavior is confirmed.

docs.title: Technology Effects
docs.text:
Technology Effect Advanced Grappling Adds Grapple duration. Advanced Grappling II Adds more Grapple duration. Advanced Grappling III Adds the highest Grapple duration bonus and lets the grappler drag the target when moving. Grapple Speed Reduces Grapple cooldown by 4 s. Grapple Speed II Reduces Grapple cooldown by 6 s. Grapple Speed III Reduces Grapple cooldown by 8 s. Reverse Grappling Gives a high chance to deflect Grapple back at attackers.

Advanced Grappling can come from the Grappling Hook special item and some ship progression sources.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Hacking | Drain | Technician | Taunt | Deflector | Junker
  • Stats -- Grapple Chance / Resist
  • Abilities

docs.title: Hacking Ability
docs.text:
With 10+ Hack Chance, a ship can use the Hack hotbar action. Hack requires a target in the same sector, is a hostile action, has a default 50 s cooldown, and costs battery to use. Hacked targets:

  • Cannot use any hotbar ability except Repair
  • Battery does not regenerate
  • Special movement (engines, Space Lanes) does not work

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
• Hack Chance increases the Hack effect.
  • Hack Resist reduces the Hack effect.
  • For player ships, Hit Chance can contribute to Hack power and Evasion can contribute to Hack resistance.
  • If the final effect is too weak, the Hack can be resisted.

Exact duration and resist formulas are Needs data until the live behavior is confirmed.

docs.title: Technology Effects
docs.text:
Technology Effect Advanced Hacking Adds Hack duration. Advanced Hacking II Adds more Hack duration. Advanced Hacking III Adds the highest Hack duration bonus and makes hacked targets lose Special Item abilities. Hack Speed Reduces Hack cooldown by 4 s. Hack Speed II Reduces Hack cooldown by 6 s. Hack Speed III Reduces Hack cooldown by 8 s. Reverse Hacking Gives a high chance to deflect Hack back at attackers.

Advanced Hacking III can disable Special Item effects such as Nova and Deflector. It also interacts with Technician repair-over-time immunity.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Grapple | Drain | Technician | Taunt | Deflector | Junker
  • Stats -- Hack Chance / Resist
  • Abilities

docs.title: Junker Technology
docs.text:
Junker can trigger a hull repair to the attacker whenever they deal damage (including drones, splash, battle ram). The attacker is repaired for 33% to 100% of the total hull damage dealt. It does not trigger if total damage is less than 30.

Junker does not benefit from other tech (e.g. Technician) or the +Hull Repair stat.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Grapple | Hacking | Drain | Technician | Taunt | Deflector
  • Abilities

docs.title: Small Strike Team Bonus
docs.text:
When a fleet of 4 or more ships has at least 33% Small or Medium ships, the fleet gets the Small Strike Team bonus:

  • +5% Outgoing Damage for the entire fleet
  • +5 to all resists for the entire fleet

The bonus applies to all ships in the fleet, not only the small/medium ones.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Ships -- Fleet size and size requirements
  • Size and Targeting -- Why small ships matter
  • Abilities

docs.title: Taunt (Tank)
docs.text:
The Tank Special item gains Taunt at Ultra-Rare rarity and above: it forces damaged enemies to attack the tank. The target change happens automatically on any HIT or CRITICAL from the tank (not on SPLASH or other damage types; it can splash during some daily events). Duration depends on the Tank item's rarity and quality.

The forced target change also triggers a new target lock cooldown for the victim, as with any target change.

docs.title: Counter
docs.text: Technician technology can remove Taunt and apply a Repair-Over-Time effect that protects the victim from negative effects for 21 seconds.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Grapple | Hacking | Drain | Technician | Deflector | Junker
  • Abilities
  • Size and Targeting -- Target lock

docs.title: Technician Technology
docs.text: Ships can improve repair ability with the Technician line from the Technician Special item or the Medic ship mod. Higher rarity on the Technician Special and higher Elite Rank on a Medic ship add more ranks.

docs.title: Ranks
docs.text:
Rank Effect 1 Remove negative status effects -- Purge button on friendly ships with a negative effect (8 s cooldown). Normal repairs do not remove negative effects. 2 Repairs can critical (use ship's Critical Chance). 3 Repairs can splash to nearby ships (use ship's Splash Chance). 4 Repair-over-time (ROT) -- 21 s, +50% hull from the repair that triggered it. Target cannot be grappled, hacked, or taunted during ROT; one of the strongest counter-strategies. First pulse removes existing negative effects.

Repair drones with Technician can remove negative effects but cannot splash or apply repair-over-time.

docs.title: Counters
docs.text: Technician can remove Taunt and apply ROT, protecting the victim for 21 seconds. Advanced Hacking III can purge ROT and hack the target.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Grapple | Hacking | Drain | Taunt | Deflector | Junker
  • Stats -- Critical Chance, Splash Chance
  • Abilities

docs.title: Activities
docs.text: Activities are the main ways you spend time in-game beyond core systems: PvP (open-world and Arena) and PvE (missions, invasions, exploration). Use the links below for details.

docs.title: PvP
docs.text:
• Player vs Player (PvP) -- Open-world PvP, territory, and Skulls.
  • The Arena -- Structured PvP with balanced teams and Skull rewards.

docs.title: PvE
docs.text:
• Player vs Environment (PvE) -- PvE content and safe maps.
  • Invasions -- Timed PvE events and Phoenix ship mod.
  • Outpost Missions -- Mission chains tied to specific outposts.

docs.title: Outpost Missions
docs.text: Outpost Missions are missions tied to a specific outpost. They can ask you to complete tasks such as killing specific NPCs or collecting and depositing resources.

docs.title: Finding the mission source
docs.text:
To identify which outpost or map a mission belongs to:

  1. Open the Missions tab.
  2. Click the question mark button.
  3. Read the header above the outpost mission.

The header shows the outpost mission source, such as the map or outpost name.

docs.title: Mission chains
docs.text:
Outpost missions can chain from one task into another. For example:

  1. Accept the outpost mission.
  2. Complete the listed kill task, such as killing a specific NPC type.
  3. Return for the next task.
  4. If the next task asks for resources, collect the resource and deposit it directly into the issuing outpost.

Resource-deposit steps must be turned in at the correct outpost. Depositing at another outpost does not complete that mission step.

docs.title: Active mission limit
docs.text: Only one outpost mission should be active at a time for the mission source you are trying to target. If you are trying to receive a specific outpost mission, avoid carrying another outpost mission that can block or confuse the chain.

docs.title: Contributor notes
docs.text:
Exact reward amounts, refresh rules, and mission-selection behavior need confirmation before adding tables. Community notes mention Tech Point rewards from outpost mission chains, including one observed Castor-chain value around 35 TP, but those values should be checked before being treated as canonical.

Needs data

Add a table of outpost mission chains only after each chain's source outpost, steps, resource deposit target, and reward are verified in game.

docs.title: See also
docs.text:
• Missions
  • Outposts
  • PvE

docs.title: Player vs Environment (PvE)
docs.text: PvE is everything you do against the world and NPCs rather than other players: fighting pirates and aliens, harvesting, missions, and events like Invasions. In human-controlled (PvE) maps you are safe from player attack -- they appear blue on the Starmap.

docs.title: What Counts as PvE
docs.text: Activity Description NPC combat Killing normal, elite, and leader NPCs; clearing NPC defense units. Harvesting Asteroids (Rock, Radioactive, Ice, Darkmatter) and resources. Missions Personal, corp, and alliance mission objectives (many are PvE). Invasions Timed PvE events; defeat invasion NPCs and bosses for rewards. Open World Farming Repeatable NPC killing, harvesting, missions, and loot. NPC Busts Group clears of NPC structures and planets. Exploring Unlocking systems, finding jump gates, and discovering the Starmap.

docs.title: Safe Space vs PvP Space
docs.text:
• PvE maps (blue) -- Human-controlled; no player attacks. Ideal for leveling, missions, and farming when you don't want PvP risk.
  • PvP maps -- Other factions; PvP is possible. Higher risk, but better pity loot, more XP, and Relics from shrines.

Daily events like Intergalactic Love Day turn off open-world PvP so everyone can focus on PvE; War Day turns it back on (reset at 00:00 UTC).

docs.title: Rewards
docs.text:
• Pity loot -- Progresses with NPC kills; higher Security Rating = better progress and tiers.
  • XP and resources -- From kills, harvesting, and mission completion.
  • Event loot -- Invasions, holiday events, and free daily gifts offer unique rewards (e.g. Phoenix ship mod, Void items).

Daily reset

Most timed PvE mechanics (new missions, free daily gifts, defense unit leveling) reset at 00:00 UTC.

docs.title: Topics
docs.text: Topic Description Open World Farming Farming loop, harvesting notes, Freighter caveat, economy cautions. NPC Busts Structure/planet clears, group calls, and cautions. Invasions PvE invasion events; bosses, victory loot, and Phoenix mod.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Missions
  • Maps & Security Rating
  • PvP -- When and where you can be attacked
  • Structures -- NPC defenses and corp defenses in PvE maps

docs.title: Invasions
docs.text: Invasions are PvE events where NPC forces attack and players work together to defeat them. Winning an invasion and killing invasion NPCs (including bosses) gives strong rewards.

docs.title: Rewards
docs.text:
Reward Notes Victory loot Invasion victory loot has a 1:20 chance to be Void. Phoenix Ship Mod You can earn the Phoenix ship mod and titles by killing Invasion NPCs. Phoenix gives Rebirth (fatal damage -> repair + cloak) and improved repairs on injured allies. Party Box activities Party Box activities can include "Invasion Victory" -- complete the task for bonus loot (e.g. 1 in 25 chance of an advanced Tech Computer).

See Void Items and Loot and Reward Reference for related reward notes.

docs.title: Pity Loot Note
docs.text: Invasion boss loot and other non-standard invasion drops do not reset your pity loot counters. Your normal pity progress is unchanged.

docs.title: Hardcore & Oblivion
docs.text:
On the Oblivion server, Hardcore ships can get up to a 10x loot bonus (or more during invasions) when they get the killing shot, stacking with other bonuses.

Event timing

Invasion events are scheduled in-game. Check the game or announcements for when invasions are active.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• PvE overview
  • Ship Mods -- Phoenix and other mods
  • Void Items
  • Pity Loot

docs.title: NPC Busts
docs.text: NPC busts are group PvE clears against NPC defense units, planets, or high-value structure targets. Players often use them for loot, credits, missions, and activity rewards.

docs.title: Rewards And Progress
docs.text: Benefit Notes Loot and scrap value NPC busts can produce items with scrap value. Exact values are market-sensitive. Mission progress NPC defense-unit kills can progress some Missions. Reward Points Some event/reward systems include NPC structure kills. See Reward Point Gifts.

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• PvE overview
  • Structures
  • Missions
  • Credits
  • NPC Bust Strategy

docs.title: Open World Farming
docs.text: Open-world farming is repeatable PvE: killing NPCs, harvesting asteroids and debris, progressing missions, and collecting loot while managing travel risk.

docs.title: Core farming loop
docs.text:
1. Pick a map you can survive in.
  2. Kill NPCs for XP, loot, mission progress, and pity loot.
  3. Harvest asteroids, debris, and resource nodes.
  4. Scrap low-value items or resources for Credits, or sell useful gear to players.
  5. Repair ships and equipment before durability becomes a problem.

docs.title: Risk and reward
docs.text: Area Tradeoff Human-controlled PvE maps Safer, lower risk, good for leveling and low-stress farming. PvP maps Higher risk, but better XP/loot incentives and faster Pity Loot progress. Higher Security Rating maps Stronger enemies and better reward potential. See Security Rating for known level bands and missing live-data fields.

docs.title: Harvesting
docs.text:
Harvester drones gather resources from asteroids and debris. Most general-purpose farming ships want at least one Harvester unless they are built for a focused combat role.

Useful harvesting notes:

  • Bonuses that increase resources harvested improve harvesting results.
  • The Drone Master special can reduce harvesting cooldown.
  • The Freighter ship mod improves harvesting, but reduces damage.

Needs data

The in-game wiki says Freighter doubles harvesting chances, while gootchat describes the ship's harvests yielding 2x normal amount. Confirm the exact current behavior before writing a precise formula.

docs.title: Economy reference
docs.text: Market values change. The wiki should avoid fixed current prices unless they are dated observations. Use the Credits page for stable currency rules and player advice for live pricing.

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• PvE overview
  • Missions
  • Pity Loot
  • Credits
  • Ship Mods
  • Wyrd
  • Open World Farming Strategy

docs.title: Player vs Player (PvP)
docs.text: PvP is where you earn Skulls, fight for territory, and help your alliance capture Relic Shrines. Human-controlled space is safe; everywhere else you can be attacked -- or attack.

docs.title: Where You're Safe (PvE Maps)
docs.text:
You are safe from attack in human-controlled systems, called PvE Maps, marked in BLUE on the Starmap.

When you join a Corporation you also join their alliance. In PvP maps you're on a team with everyone in that alliance. Friendly ships share vision, can follow or repair each other, and attack enemies to split XP and loot.

Shared target, attack, and hostile ability rules are covered on Combat and Targeting.

docs.title: Why PvP? Rewards
docs.text:
• ~10% more XP and ~20% more loot when hunting in PvP maps (vs safe space).
  • Pity loot progresses faster in dangerous areas.
  • Skulls -- from killing players and from defending or attacking non-NPC defense units. Structure warfare is a major source of Skulls.
  • Relics -- Most PvP maps have a Shrine. Corps place a Garrison on the shrine to capture the map and generate Relics for the alliance.

docs.title: Where You're (Mostly) Safe in PvP
docs.text:
• At your corp's defense units -- You're relatively safe until that structure comes under attack.
  • At Jump Gates -- Safe only if you're not flagged in combat.

Never 100% safe

You are never 100% safe in PvP maps. Even at your own structures, an attack can start at any time.

docs.title: Collision (Stacking Limit)
docs.text: A Collision mechanic limits how many ships can be in one sector. See Sectors for the canonical limits.

docs.title: Fleet operations
docs.text: Large PvP fights use drivers, assist leaders, and shared sort calls as gameplay/UI concepts. See Fleet PvP for the rules summary and Fleet PvP Quick Start for cattabliss' practical guide.

docs.title: Love Day (No Open-World PvP)
docs.text: Intergalactic Love Day is a daily event that disables open-world PvP. On Love Days everyone can work together. It alternates with War Day (PvP on) at each daily reset (00:00 UTC).

docs.title: PvP Activities
docs.text: Activity Description The Arena Controlled PvP matches every few hours; awards Skulls and has no ship durability loss. Fleet PvP Open-world organized PvP with drivers, assist leaders, and sort calls. Open-world PvP Fight enemy players in PvP maps. Structure Warfare Rules Attack or defend defense units; bonus Skulls for both.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:

  • PvP Basics -- Practical first PvP habits
  • Fleet PvP -- Drivers, assist leaders, sort calls, and collision management
  • Structure Warfare Rules -- Structure warfare rules
  • Combat and Targeting -- Target locks, attack readiness, and hostile ability gates

docs.title: The Arena
docs.text: The Arena is a controlled PvP environment with balanced teams, no Artifacts, and no Corporation Skills. Arena matches award Skulls and other rewards.

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
• Schedule -- Rounds run every few hours at the top of the hour. Registration opens before each round.
  • Access -- Captain Panel -> ARENA when registration is announced.
  • Objective -- The team with the most kills wins. Captains who lose but have high kill counts still get bonus rewards.
  • No durability loss -- Your ships don't lose durability on death in The Arena. Gear loses only 1% durability, and only half the time.

docs.title: Queue Rules
docs.text:
Arena queue validation checks the selected ship before it can enter a round.

Requirement Rule Ship state Destroyed ships, NPC ships, Corp Defenders, ships listed for sale, and ships listed for trade cannot join. Arena type Skirmish Arena requires a Skirmisher ship. Skirmisher ships cannot join non-Skirmish rounds. Hardcore type Hardcore rounds require Hardcore ships. Non-Hardcore rounds reject Hardcore ships. Gear The ship must have at least one Weapon or Fighter Drone equipped and cannot use free starter items. Rarity Basic-rarity gear is blocked, excluding Specials and Engines. Empty slots Ships with enough captain level for the gear check can have at most one empty accessible gear slot. Hardcore artifact rule Hardcore Arena blocks Weird Alien Artifacts. Gear Score At least 1,000 Gear Score is required to queue. Full-reward thresholds may be higher and should be checked on the in-game Arena screen. Level requirement If the selected ship has a required player level, the captain must meet it.

Daily Arena events can add extra queue restrictions, such as blocking Huge and Massive ships.

For shared target, attack, and ability gates, see Combat and Targeting.

Needs confirmation

Reward values and damage participation thresholds below remain community values unless confirmed by the in-game Arena screen.

docs.title: Modes And Modifiers
docs.text: Type Notes Standard Bring up to 3 ships; following teammates is not allowed. Fleet Bring up to 4 ships; following teammates is allowed. Hardcore Bring up to 3 Hardcore ships; following teammates is not allowed; gives extra Skulls. Chaos Modifier with smaller/more teams and item rewards weighted toward individual kill count. Ultimate Modifier where gear becomes Ultimate rarity for the duration of the Arena.

docs.title: Observed reward notes
docs.text:
Community notes give these observed reward values:

Result or bonus Skulls Losing team 7 Winning team 15 Hardcore Arena +2 Chaos Arena +2 Arena Awards Bonus Loot and Skulls daily event +2 and an additional item reward for winning Last team standing +3 Underdog team +3 Team MVP category +4, with +1 for each additional MVP category

Players on a losing team can still earn bonus rewards for strong performance, such as high kill count, top healing, or utility.

Needs confirmation

Arena reward values and thresholds can change. Check the in-game Arena screen when exact rewards matter.

docs.title: Arena Rewards Summary
docs.text: Arena can award Skulls, items, and performance bonuses. Exact reward values can change, so check the in-game Arena screen when the exact payout matters.

docs.title: Player Quick Starts
docs.text:
• Arena Quick Start -- cattabliss' practical entry guide.
  • Lucky's Arena Notes -- community Arena notes.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Skulls -- What Arena Skulls are spent on
  • Player vs Player -- Open-world PvP and structure warfare
  • Ships -- Fleet and Gear Score
  • Items -- Gear Score and equipment
  • Combat and Targeting -- Target locks, attack readiness, and hostile ability gates

docs.title: Fleet PvP Rules
docs.text:
Fleet PvP is open-world player combat organized around drivers, assist leaders, collision limits, and shared target sorting.

For community tactics, see Fleet PvP Strategy. For cattabliss' authored guide material, see Fleet PvP Quick Start.

docs.title: Drivers and assist leaders
docs.text:
A driver is the ship a fleet follows. Fleet calls often link drivers in chat as D1, D2, D3, or similar labels.

Use My Assist Leader on a linked driver ship to:

  • Bring that ship to the top of your allied ship list while it is in sector.
  • Mark it with a yellow square on the map.
  • Make it easier to jump to the fleet and follow the correct ship.

If a driver dies or gets too far away, players may need to refollow another driver. Follow distance rules live on Sectors.

docs.title: Collision
docs.text: Fleet stacks are subject to collision. Local sector ship counts can be enabled in settings; see Recommended Settings.

docs.title: Sort calls and targeting UI
docs.text:
Enemy sort orders make allied target lists display in the same order.

The mechanics page should only document that sort controls exist and why fleets use shared sort calls. Specific sort-call choices and driver-building advice belong in Fleet PvP Strategy.

docs.title: Interdictors
docs.text: Interdictors can slow enemy movement and targeting in affected sectors. Move-speed bonuses can offset Interdictor movement penalties, and Gravity Drives negate the movement penalty for ships that equip them.

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Player vs Player
  • Sectors
  • Skulls
  • Carrier Leadership
  • Interdictor Carrier
  • Fleet PvP Strategy
  • Fleet PvP Quick Start

docs.title: Structure Warfare Rules
docs.text:
Structure busts are organized PvP attacks against non-NPC corporation defense units: Planets, Garrisons, Outposts, and Shipyards.

This page is for game rules and reference logic. For community tactics, see Structure Busting Strategy.

For shared attack requirements and target rules, see Combat and Targeting.

docs.title: Stable rules
docs.text: Rule Notes Structure Gear Score Player defense units need 3,000+ Gear Score or they are recalled. Attack shield When a defense unit is attacked, it gets a 90-second immunity shield so defenders can organize. Break shield After enough attackers die, a 1-hour protection shield can be raised. Alliance shield Alliance leaders can use :ALLIANCE SHIELD for a 4-hour shield once per day. Quick Jump Players can Quick Jump to some structures under attack. Outpost behavior and alliance-owned edge cases need in-game confirmation. Blocking Quick Jump Engaging an enemy defense unit may prevent defenders from Quick Jumping to it. Needs confirmation.

docs.title: Skull payout
docs.text:
When a non-NPC structure is destroyed in PvP, attackers earn Skulls based on the structure's Gear Score:

Structure Payout Outpost / Shipyard 1 Skull per 500 Gear Score Garrison 1 Skull per 400 Gear Score Planet 1 Skull per 300 Gear Score

See Skulls for other PvP earning rules.

docs.text:

docs.title: Events
docs.text: Timed and daily content: free gifts, daily event modifiers, holiday gems, and Party Boxes. Most reset at 00:00 UTC each day.

docs.title: Daily & Free
docs.text: Topic Description Free Daily Gifts FREE button at defense units, GIFT BOX in menu, weekly Void reward. Daily Events 50+ random modifiers (crafting bonuses, small-ship buffs, Love Day / War Day). Type :DAILY in chat.

docs.title: Special & Rewards
docs.text: Topic Description Holiday Events Gem events on set dates; kill gem NPCs for rare loot. Clues every 30 minutes. Party Boxes Buffs and activities for corp/alliance; from Reward Point Gifts or weekly gift. Reward Point Gifts Earned from Tech Point purchases, NPC structure kills, PvP. Spend on Party Boxes, Deception Module, skins, and more.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Missions -- Reset with daily
  • PvE -- Event loot and modifiers
  • Currencies -- Tech Points and Star Shards

docs.title: Daily Events
docs.text:
There is a daily event reset every 24 hours. Most timed mechanics reset at 00:00 Coordinated Universal Time (UTC), including:

  • Leveling defense units
  • New missions
  • Free Daily Gifts

docs.title: Random Daily Events
docs.text:
Over 50 random Daily Events can modify core game rules, for example:

  • Granting small ships powerful temporary abilities
  • Adding bonus stats to any crafting that day (ships or items)

You get an alert when logging in with the current daily events, or type :DAILY in chat at any time.

docs.title: Love Day and War Day
docs.text:
Intergalactic Love Day and War Day alternate with each daily reset:

  • Love Day -- Open-world PvP is disabled. All players can cooperate.
  • War Day -- Open-world PvP is enabled.

docs.title: "Aliens" in Events and Missions
docs.text: In daily events and missions, "Aliens" means Wyrd, Precursor, and Hets -- not Pirates. Missions that say "Aliens" can include Pirates depending on the mission text.

docs.title: Example: Ancient Alien Secrets Hidden In Asteroids
docs.text:
When this event is active:

  • Only real asteroids count (Rock, Radioactive, Ice, Darkmatter).
  • Less risk = less reward -- better results in PvP maps.
  • Primary way Special Items enter the game (via deconstruct and blueprints).
  • Bonuses that increase resources harvested improve chances.
  • Freighter ship mod improves harvesting; exact doubling behavior needs confirmation before using it as a formula.
  • Drone Master special reduces harvesting cooldown.

docs.title: Secret Unlocks
docs.text:
Some events require secret unlocks involving your portrait or selected title, e.g.:

  • Naked Aggression
  • Young is Brash - Old is Wise - Admirals are Critical
  • Space Karen Demands Resources

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Free Daily Gifts
  • Missions
  • PvP -- Love Day / War Day
  • Holiday Events

docs.title: Free Daily Gifts
docs.text: Players can return each day to claim several rewards. Daily gifts reset at 00:00 Coordinated Universal Time (UTC) every day.

docs.title: How to Claim
docs.text: Gift How Crafting resources Use the FREE button at any defense unit (your corp's or discovered NPC). GIFT BOX Hamburger menu (drop-down) -> GIFT BOX. Randomly upgrades ships Elite Ranks, equipped item rarity or quality, durability repairs, and more. Weekly gift (Void items) Captain Panel -> REWARDS -> CLAIM WEEKLY GIFT. Earn powerful Void items by completing weekly Party Box missions.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Daily Events -- Same 00:00 UTC reset
  • Party Boxes -- Weekly missions and Void rewards
  • Reward Point Gifts -- Other reward track

docs.title: Holiday Events
docs.text: On specific calendar days, special gem holiday events run. During these events, certain NPCs drop rare and powerful items.

docs.title: Event Dates
docs.text: Date Holiday January 1st New Year March 17th St. Patrick's Day June 19th Juneteenth July 4th Independence Day October 31st Halloween 4th Thursday of November Thanksgiving December 25th Christmas

docs.title: How It Works
docs.text:
• Gem NPCs -- Special NPCs have a glowing gem floating over their ship. Kill them to collect the drop.
  • Spawn -- Gems can spawn anywhere at any Security Rating, but often in harder areas.
  • Clues -- Every 30 minutes, a clue about where to find gems is whispered to one player. Sometimes a whole corporation or even the entire universe is told.
  • Visibility -- Gems are not visible for 30 minutes (even to the player who got the clue), giving captains time to reach the spot before others.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Free Daily Gifts
  • Daily Events
  • Reward Point Gifts

docs.title: Party Boxes
docs.text:
Party Boxes are items that grant your corporation or alliance a temporary activity or combat bonus. They mainly come from Reward Point Gifts and are thrown to a random sector when used. Players who receive a party cannot loot another party box for 1 hour.

Captain Panel -> REWARDS -> My Party Bonus to see your active buffs and activities. They expire after 21 days.

docs.title: Party Buffs
docs.text:
Grants a boost to the next attack or ability. Cannot be paused or used in The Arena.

Buff Effect Critical Attacks Overpower Advanced Grapple Attacks Overpower Advanced Hack Attacks Overpower Advanced Resource Harvests Overpower Advanced Hull Repairs Overpower Advanced

docs.title: Party Activities
docs.text:
Complete the task to earn bonus loot. 1 in 25 chance of an advanced Tech Computer!

Activity Task Invasion Victory Win an invasion. NPC Defense Unit Bust Destroy NPC defense units. Complete Missions Finish missions. Kill Assassin Leaders Kill Assassin-class NPC leaders. Hunt Advanced Pirates or Aliens Hunt Parties require killing Elite Rank 5 (ADV) enemies. Must grant you XP; cannot be part of an invasion or defense engagement.

docs.title: Void Items from Weekly Gift
docs.text: Captains can earn powerful Void items via the Free Weekly Gift (Captain Panel -> REWARDS -> CLAIM WEEKLY GIFT) when Party Box missions are completed.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Reward Point Gifts -- How to get Party Boxes
  • Free Daily Gifts -- Weekly gift claim
  • Invasions -- Invasion Victory activity

docs.title: Reward Point Gifts
docs.text: Reward Points are earned by supporting the game and by certain in-game actions. They unlock bonus gifts (Party Boxes, modules, skins, and more). Open Captain Panel -> REWARDS for a summary of your points and prizes.

docs.title: How to Earn Reward Points
docs.text: Source Reward Points Tech Point purchases Every 1 Tech Point = 1 Reward Point. Buy at Spellbook.com. Destroying NPC defense structures Planets: 25. Garrisons: 8. Outposts or Shipyards: 4. Open-world PvP Making the killing shot in PvP (non-Arena).

docs.title: Gifts You Can Claim
docs.text:
Reward Cost Notes Deception Module 1,000 Hides gear and stats from other players; works with other ship mods. Captain title -- Exclusive title. Retro Ship Skins -- Exclusive skins. Special random bonus 5,000 Unlock at 5,000 Reward Points. Re-Mutate Item Kit 3,000 Upgrades and re-rolls a mutated item (Captain Panel -> REWARDS). Party Boxes -- Use to gift corp or alliance activities and combat bonuses.

Supporting the game

Tech Point purchases help cover server and development costs. Reward Points are a way to thank players for continued support.

docs.text:

docs.title: Getting Started
docs.text: New to The Infinite Black 2? This section covers the basics so you can get into the game quickly.

docs.title: Start Here
docs.text:
• How To Play -- What the game is, how to move, your first goals, and why joining a corporation matters.
  • Common Workflows -- Where to click for settings, gifts, repairs, Arena, Quick Jump, and fleet assist.
  • Recommended Settings -- Settings that make sectors, PvP, and ship lists easier to read.
  • Chat Commands -- Common commands, with caveats.
  • FAQ -- Common questions: getting better, Equip Points, chat commands, skills, durability, Skulls, and more.

docs.title: Other Resources
docs.text:
• Player-Written Guides -- Community guides and external links.
  • Quick Start Guides -- cattabliss systems guides and LuckyMFer's Arena notes.
  • Corporations -- How corps work and why you should join one early.
  • Maps -- Navigating sectors and security ratings.
  • Device Sharing & Multiple Accounts -- One account per player; enforcement.
  • Delete Account -- How to delete your Spellbook account.

Stuck?

Type :HELP in chat for a list of in-game commands. Reach out to Goot in-game for wiki questions or suggestions!

docs.title: Chat Commands
docs.text:
Many TIB2 features can be run from chat. Type :HELP in game for the full current command list.

Command verification

Chat commands can change. Treat this page as a quick reference and verify expensive commands in game before using them.

docs.title: Common commands
docs.text:
Command Use Notes :HELP Opens command/help information. Best source for the complete current list. freegift Level 25 gift command. Observed rewards include a Mark upgrade kit, a Victor Mark CPU, and a Skip Drive; you may need to use the code twice to receive the Mark upgrade kit. Consult allies before using or selling the kit. :ARENA Arena access shortcut. Also available from the player/Captain panel when registration is open. :corp repair mytp confirm Corporation item repair. Repairs 5 random equipped items by 10% for every online corp member and spends Tech Points. See Item Repair Quick Start. :alliance repair mytp confirm Alliance item repair. Repairs 2 random equipped items by 10% for every online alliance member and spends Tech Points. :CRAFT Crafting management. Used for crafting queue and related item-crafting commands. :CRAFTVOID Void crafting management. Used by crafters for Void Items.

Corp repair spelling

Some older guide text says :corp repair my tp confirm. Current wiki references use :corp repair mytp confirm, matching the later repair guide. Verify in game before spending Tech Points.

docs.title: Chat quality-of-life
docs.text: The R hotkey can quickly open chat and start a reply to the last player you privately messaged or received a private message from. You can also open chat from the top-left button and use the reply button beside the text entry field.

docs.title: Related
docs.text:

docs.title: Common Workflows
docs.text: Use this page for "where do I click?" basics. Deeper mechanics live on the linked canonical pages.

docs.title: First hour
docs.text: Goal Where to go Start moving Click adjacent sectors on the map. See Sectors. Follow the first mission Open the mission prompt and explore 100% of Sol while fighting pirates and harvesting asteroids. See Missions. Deploy your second ship After earning another ship, click Deploy 2nd Ship. See Ships. Join a corporation Open corporation options from the player/captain UI and find an active corp. See Corporations.

docs.title: Settings
docs.text:
Goal Path Show sector ship counts Hamburger menu -> Settings -> Gameplay -> Gameplay Settings -> Display local sector ship counts. Show sector names Hamburger menu -> Settings -> Gameplay -> Gameplay Settings -> Always show sector name text. Sort enemies in PvP Hamburger menu -> Sort Enemy -> choose the called sort. Show players over NPCs Ship button list settings -> Show player ships over NPC ships.

See Recommended Settings for why these matter.

docs.title: Gifts and rewards
docs.text:
Goal Path Claim daily crafting resources Click FREE at a discovered defense unit. Claim the daily Gift Box Hamburger menu -> GIFT BOX. Claim weekly Void reward Captain Panel -> REWARDS -> CLAIM WEEKLY GIFT. Check Reward Points and Party Box bonuses Captain Panel -> REWARDS. Level 25 gift Type freegift in chat. See Chat Commands.

See Free Daily Gifts and Reward Point Gifts.

docs.title: Items and repairs
docs.text: Goal Path Upgrade rarity or rank Click item -> Upgrade. See Item Upgrades. Manage item restrictions See Item Management for repair, scrap, deconstruct, auction, and corp-bank rules. Repair an item from the item panel Click item -> Upgrade -> Repair. This can reduce quality. Repair through corp/alliance chat Use :corp repair mytp confirm or :alliance repair mytp confirm. See Chat Commands. Repair ship durability Click ship -> REPAIR. This spends Skulls.

docs.title: Travel and PvP
docs.text: Goal Path Open the Starmap Hamburger menu -> Starmap. Quick Jump Hamburger menu -> Quick Jump target. Costs 1 Tech Point. Join Arena Captain Panel -> ARENA, or use :ARENA when registration is open. Assist a fleet driver Click linked driver ship -> My Assist Leader, then follow the marked ship. See Fleet PvP.

docs.text:

docs.title: Delete My Account
docs.text:
You can delete your Spellbook account at any time.

  • Spellbook does not store personal information or payment data.
  • To delete your account, visit: https://www.TIB2.com/delete

Back to Getting Started

docs.title: Device Sharing and Multiple Accounts
docs.text: One Spellbook account per player. You cannot have two or more accounts under any circumstances, even if you use multiple devices.

docs.title: Enforcement
docs.text:
Breaking this rule results in ALL accounts permanently banned -- including accounts used by roommates, family members, or others on the same device or network.

The "One Account" rule keeps the game fair. Multiple accounts ruined the original Infinite Black; the same policy applies here.

docs.title: If You Believe It's a Mistake
docs.text:
Email Contact@Spellbook.com for help. Be aware: they will ask for proof.

Data we use

Spellbook does not collect personal information. They only see data similar to a website: e.g. IP address, game client, and similar technical data.

Back to Getting Started

docs.title: Frequently Asked Questions
docs.text: Common questions from new and returning players. If you don't see your question here, check How To Play or type :HELP in chat.

docs.title: I'm a new player! How do I play? What should I do and where do I start?
docs.text: Start with How To Play, then use Common Workflows when you need exact click paths.

docs.title: This game is hard -- How do I get better?
docs.text:
Combat is balanced around multiple ships. Playing with a team and deploying additional ships is critical to success!

  1. Explore 100% of the Sol Star System to earn your first ship.
  2. Click "Deploy 2nd Ship" to grow your fleet.
  3. Join a Corporation -- they provide Technology, Skills, and group protection.

docs.title: What are Equip Points and how do I get more?
docs.text: See Equip Points. That page is the canonical reference for capacity, item cost, and missing data.

docs.title: What chat commands can I use?
docs.text: Several key features use chat commands. Type :HELP into chat for the full list, and see Chat Commands for common commands.

docs.title: What settings should I turn on?
docs.text: Turn on Display local sector ship counts early. It helps you avoid collision in fleet play and read friendly/enemy presence in a sector. See Recommended Settings for the rest of the new-player settings pass.

docs.title: What are drones?
docs.text: See Drones for Fighter, Harvester, and Repair drone roles, and Equipment Slots for first-slot vs auxiliary-slot behavior.

docs.title: What skills should I get first?
docs.text: For the rules, see Skills: corporation, captain, and ship skills all stack. For recommendations, ask your corporation or use the player-written quick starts and strategy pages.

docs.title: How do I get more Ship XP, Player XP, or Corp XP?
docs.text: Technology, Skills, and Items all stack to give your ships a +XP% Bonus. When you kill an NPC you gain XP based on your ship's level vs the target's level. A share of that goes to your player, and a share to your corporation -- so boosting ship XP boosts all three.

docs.title: What is Ship Durability?
docs.text: When ships are destroyed they can lose a small amount of durability. Repair ship durability with Skulls using the REPAIR button on the ship inspection panel. See Stats for affected stat definitions.

docs.title: How do I get Skulls?
docs.text: Skulls are earned only from Player vs Player Combat. See the Skulls article for details and The Arena as the main way to earn them.

docs.title: What is Item Durability?
docs.text: See Item Upgrades for item repair paths, Item Management for restrictions, and Item Repair Quick Start before spending Tech Points on large repairs.

docs.title: What ship should I build first?
docs.text: Ship construction rules are on Ship Crafting, and ship requirements are on Ship Reference. Build recommendations belong in your corporation's advice, Ship Building Strategy, or Build Philosophy.

docs.title: Why do my ship stats seem lower than they should be?
docs.text: See Stat Bonus Diminishing Returns. Stats use diminishing returns so stacking one stat gives less benefit the more you have.

docs.title: How can I support this game?
docs.text: The best way is to Review The Game on your platform's store. It helps the indie developers a lot!

docs.title: How To Play
docs.text:
Getting started in The Infinite Black 2 is easy!

You are the captain of a small spaceship exploring the universe: killing pirates, gathering resources, and looting treasure.

docs.title: The Universe = Sectors
docs.text:
The universe is represented by a map of connected squares, called sectors.

Think of a sector like a room: inside you will find enemies to kill, asteroids to harvest, and other players. Travel from sector to sector by clicking on the squares to plot a course.

docs.title: Your First Goals
docs.text:
1. Follow your first Mission: explore the Sol Star System while fighting pirates and harvesting asteroids.
  2. Explore 100% of Sol to earn your first extra ship.
  3. Click "Deploy 2nd Ship" to grow your fleet.
  4. Join a Corporation so you can get advice, group protection, technology, skills, and later Carrier benefits.

docs.title: Scale and Difficulty
docs.text: The universe is huge -- over 30,000 sectors to explore. Enemies become more difficult in higher Security Rating maps, where players fight for territory with their corporation.

docs.title: Join a Corporation Early
docs.text:
Joining a Corporation early is very important! Corps provide:

  • Technology benefits
  • Advice and hunting groups
  • Access to advanced ship construction (shipyards)

If your corporation isn't helpful, find one that is -- they benefit from you joining too.

docs.title: Early milestones
docs.text: Milestone Why it matters Level 25 The freegift chat command has been observed giving a Mark upgrade kit, Victor Mark CPU, and Skip Drive. See Chat Commands before using it. Level 35 Unlocks Carrier use. Carrier Leadership can make fleet movement easier, but requires being in a corporation. For build guidance, see Carrier Builds.

docs.title: First settings to check
docs.text: Turn on Display local sector ship counts and Always show sector name text early. These make map calls, missions, and future fleet play much easier to understand. See Recommended Settings.

docs.title: Daily habits
docs.text:
Check gifts and rewards early:

  • FREE button at discovered defense units for daily crafting resources.
  • Hamburger menu -> GIFT BOX for the daily Gift Box.
  • Captain Panel -> REWARDS -> CLAIM WEEKLY GIFT for weekly Void rewards when available.

See Common Workflows and Free Daily Gifts.

docs.title: Player-Written Guides
docs.text:
Community-written guides can provide extra tips and opinions. They may not always be up-to-date with the latest patch.

Guides in progress

We're collecting and linking player guides here. Official in-game links:

  • Quick Start Guides -- cattabliss systems guides and LuckyMFer's Arena notes.
  • SaltyGrease New Player Guide -- Steam Community
  • Bubbadoge's All-Inclusive Guide -- Spellbook files
  • Bubbadoge's Gear Guide -- Spellbook files

The developers pay Tech Points for well-written, comprehensive guides!

Back to Getting Started

docs.title: Review The Game
docs.text:
The Infinite Black 2 is a Spellbook indie game -- a single programmer and small team. Reviews help the game survive and reach more players.

Where to review:

  • Apple iOS App Store
  • Android Google Play
  • Steam

No compensation

There is never any compensation for reviews, positive or otherwise.

Stay up-to-date via Facebook and Discord.

Back to Getting Started

docs.title: Recommended Settings
docs.text: Some settings make the game easier to read, especially once you join a corporation or start participating in PvP.

docs.title: Gameplay settings
docs.text:
Open these from Hamburger menu -> Settings -> Gameplay -> Gameplay Settings.

Setting Recommendation Why it matters Display local sector ship counts On Shows local friendly, neutral, PvP enemy, and NPC counts. This helps prevent collision and helps read enemy presence. Always show sector name text On Makes sector coordinates visible on the main screen, which helps with mission targets, boss locations, and fleet calls. UTC time/local time Player preference The game operates on UTC. Daily reset is at 00:00 UTC, and event rolls commonly use UTC times. Camera snap back delay Player preference Controls how quickly the camera returns to your ships after you scroll away on the map. Top down/Side View Player preference Lets you choose between a flatter map view and a more angled view.

docs.title: Ship button list
docs.text:
Open these from the ship button list settings.

Sort allied ships most in danger On Helps repair/support pilots find endangered friendly ships. Ship Buttons -> show buttons Usually always, except resources Keeps common actions visible without extra clicking.

docs.title: Spellbook
docs.text:
The Infinite Black 2 is made by Spellbook -- an independent game creator in the United States. The team is one programmer, one artist, and one customer support.

Spellbook started making mobile games in 2011. The original Infinite Black has been joined by over 1.5 million players. The studio self-publishes and funds development without outside production, advertisers, or marketing.

Support the game by reviewing it on your platform's store.

Back to Getting Started

docs.title: Arena Quick Start
docs.text:
By cattabliss. LuckyMFer helped with the arena section; see Lucky's Arena Notes.

What do I do in arena Arena is a game mode that activates once per hour, at the top of the hour. (example: 00:00, 04:00, etc) Typically, players are given 3 minutes to register. You can register for arena by clicking your player name, then clicking arena, during the registration window. You will be given the chance to select anywhere from two to four ships to participate in the arena.

Once you enter arena, you will be placed onto a random team. Carriers do not work in arena, and it is almost a requirement that all ships are geared in a way to allow them to move at least at 3.5 seconds per move, or faster. This can be achieved by using movespeed void gear, using a crux drive, or a skip drive.

Fleet arenas allow follows and generally is made up of three or fewer teams. Each team will typically designate a driver by linking a ship in chat. Follow that ship.

Chaos arenas are typically very small teams, spread out all over the arena battleground.

Standard arenas are typically a few big fleets, but there is no follow permitted, and everyone must move on their own.

Ultimate arenas are a modifier on the above three arenas, except all your gear turns into ultimate rarity for the duration of the arena.

TIPS: The best items you can bring as a new player to the arena is a crux drive, and/or a stalker. The crux allows you to keep up with the rest, the stalker will allow you to go invisible when targeted, extending your survival.

docs.title: Arena Requirements
docs.text: You must have a minimum of 1,500 gear score to receive full rewards in the arena. No "new player items" are permitted in the arena. You must do a minimum of approximately 1,500 damage to gain skulls in arena. In general, losing an arena will reward you with 7 skulls, while winning will get you 15, with more skulls being awarded to team MVPs who deal the most damage, make the most repairs, have the most abilities used, or make the most kills.

docs.title: Arena Gear
docs.text:
Total hull or shield is one of the most important assets in arena. Small HP pools get targeted first. One trick some players use is to bring a very large shield, which allows you to wear a small hull and possibly maintain a 3.5s ship using cheap movespeed items. Your other option is to obtain a crux drive or skip drive (depletable, cannot be repaired outside of upgrade bonuses) and wear a large hull.

I recommend a hull or shield pool of at least 8,000, even 10,000 if possible. It will help you survive longer. The other parts would be your guns and your fighters. Make sure you've got them equipped and your offensive stats -- crit chance, hit chance, damage/hull damage buff -- will help you do sufficient damage.

Lastly, your special in arena is probably the most important item you can bring. An advanced shield with a 12k shield can make it significantly larger, making you less likely to be targeted. A technician can help heal and degrapple yourself to escape. A stalker is extremely cheap and can help you vanish if you get focus targeted. Consider your choice and make a strategy to survive and do damage.
