docs.title: Computer Deconstruction Quick Start
docs.text:
By cattabliss.

Deconstructing CPUs (computers) You will notice some players will give advice not to scrap CPUs (computers, cabal MK, havok MK, toil MK, sage MK, warrior MK, etc). The reason for this is because when 1,000 points of computers of the same type are deconstructed, the game will grant you a valuable item that can upgrade a Tech 1 computer to a Tech 2 computer, and even a Tech 2 computer to a Tech 3 computer. Tech computers are similar to MK computers, but grant + equip point bonuses and higher positive stats than their MK counterparts. There are three ranks of tech computers, Tech I, Tech II and Tech III, with the higher numbers granting additional EP points and higher stat buffs. Initially, you might think, why not deconstruct every CPU to generate these kits? There's a good reason why. A few things to note:

  1. Every computer deconstructed will grant 1 point (towards 1,000) for every rarity rank towards that class of computer, in addition to an equal number of points towards a victor computer. So, a common computer grants 1 point, an uncommon grants 2 points, a rare grants 3, and so on.
  2. The first FOUR times you hit 1,000 points of any category, you will receive a kit that will upgrade a computer from Tech 1 to Tech 2. Every FIFTH time you hit 1,000 points of any category, you will be given the initial Tech 1 to 2 kit, PLUS a kit that increases Tech 2 to Tech 3.
  3. The key to all this is to ensure that the FIFTH unlock is of a computer type that you desire. It sounds easier said than done. That's because, every time you deconstruct a CPU, you will also add points to the Victor-type computer, which most players will agree is the least useful. You want to avoid inadvertently unlocking a victor.
  4. You can check your progress in CPU deconstruction points by clicking your player name > crafting > blueprint unlock progress. The information is at the very top.

Deconstructing computers require significant planning for one simple reason. Every time you deconstruct ANY CPU, you will add points to the class of CPU you deconstructed, in addition to points to the "Victor" class. This means that if you deconstruct EVERY computer, the Victor class will unlock at a rate of up to 6 times faster than any other computer, potentially spoiling the timing of your desired unlock.

To help mitigate the impact of the victor unlocks, many players will often deconstruct their desired CPU until they are around 20 to 50 points away from 1,000. At that point, they will deconstruct other computers until their FOURTH kit is unlocked. Then, they will finish the final 20 to 50 points of their desired computer for the FIFTH. Since it is extremely important to ensure every FIFTH (so, 5, 10, 15, 20, etc) unlock is of a desired computer, you should also regularly check which unlock you have already reached. You can do this in the player stats menu. Click your name > click stats at the bottom, then scroll down.

docs.title: Credits Quick Start
docs.text:
By cattabliss.

Market-sensitive guide

This page preserves community guide advice. Any specific credit values are historical guide context, not current market prices.

How do I get more credits? Credits are one of the three main currencies, alongside skulls and tech points, are the best asset in all of TIB2. TIB2 is a deflationary economy, this means credit really is king, and holding onto credit means you will likely be able to buy more tomorrow with your money than you can today, as items become more common and their value decreases relative to the main currency. There are a few ways to get more credits:

  • Scrap items. Almost everything you can loot in the game that is not void, and not ultimate, should be scrapped, unless it is better than your current equipped items. There is generally no reason to keep an excess amount of low quality gear. Scrap it and turn it into money, which will buy you more gear later.
  • Scrap resources. For those who played TIB1, you might be confused by how resources are not automatically sold into credits, and are instead deposited. You can access your deposited resources by clicking your player name menu > crafting > stacks > creating stacks. The created stack of resources can then be sold onto the market, or scrapped for credits. Metals are the most commonly dropped resource, and are the best to scrap for credits.
  • Sell items on the market. Any player can list almost any item (with some limited exceptions) onto the market, for any price as an immediate buyout, or as an auction with a starting bid and an optional buy-out price. TIB2 does not have a market history, so the best way to figure out what to list an item for is to ask.
  • Join planet / structure busts Higher level players will regularly make calls in alliance chat for "planet" or "uni/structure/clear" busts, typically linking a driver ship and a location to jump to. Attending these is one of the most effective ways to gain credits -- each structure kill nets a UR item worth 2,000+ credit scrap, and each planet nets a legendary item worth 5,000+ scrap. 7,000+ hull is recommended for busts.

docs.title: Fleet PvP Quick Start
docs.text:
By cattabliss.

Joining PVP -- fleet vs fleet Fleet versus fleet PVP often happens spontaneously, or when fleet commanders call for players to stage in alliance chat. Typically, a call will consist of a commander/leader (highlighted chat) posting a link to a structure, and a link to a ship. Your job when joining is to ensure you follow a few rules that will help ensure a good experience for everyone on your team.

  1. Turn on your sector ship counts. There is a maximum of 75 allied ships permitted in a single sector, any higher and every ship in the fleet will lose HP until enough ships die to get back to under 75. Do not be the player to make the stack exceed 75. To turn on sector ship counts, visit the three line menu (top left) > settings > gameplay > gameplay settings > display local sector ship counts. Make sure this is checked off in green.
  2. Set assist lead on up to three drivers. Fleet vs fleet only happens with drivers. These are the ships being linked in alliance chat when a call for PVP is issued. Typically, drivers will list themselves by number: D1, D2, D3, to represent drivers 1, 2 and 3 respectively. Click each ship link, and to the right, click "my assist leader". This will highlight the ship on the map in a yellow square, and also bring the ship on top of any stack of ships you are present in, allowing you to easily follow this ship or find it on the map.

  3. Follow sort orders During PVP, there is a lot going on and the primary method most players communicate by, including commanders issuing orders, is alliance chat. Pay attention to sort calls. Common sort calls can include:

  4. small hulls

  5. fastest movement
  6. largest size
  7. most in danger
  8. worst gear

... and so on. Selecting the sort that is called ensures that the order you see enemy ships in is the same order your alliance mates see them. It helps us all shoot the same target. To select your sort, click the three line menu (top left) > sort enemy > click the called sort.

docs.title: How do I make sure my ships are geared for PVP?
docs.text:
Fleet PVP is not the same as arena PVP, but there are ways to adjust your gear to ensure you get skulls and stay alive to do so. First, ensure you have at least 10,000 hull. The most common sort is "smallest hull" and newer players tend to get targeted first, and eliminiated quickly. By having a sufficient hull point pool, you can mitigate this.

The next most important stat is targeting speed. As long as you are able to hit an enemy before it dies, you will receive skulls for the kill. There are times when you target an enemy ship, and the ship dies before you can fire a shot. This mean you won't get skulls for the kill. The solution for this is targeting speed, which can be adjusted by items such as moltic batteries, graviton/matter reverter/helical railgun, arsenal storages, and drone master specials, or through void items with a ^ modifier for targeting speed. The max targeting speed bonus is 65%, reaching this or exceeding it (up to 95% to neutralize enemy interdictor debuffs) will help you achieve kills faster and earn more skulls.

docs.title: Item Repair Quick Start
docs.text:
By cattabliss.

Repairing items: how to not lose quality There are 2 primary ways to repair items in TIB2.

1) The first way is through the item upgrade menu. Click any item, click upgrade, and hit repair. This will subtract a small number of Tech points from your wallet and repair the item to full durability at the cost of a small amount of quality. The vast majority of players do not use this method, as quality, once reduced, can typically not be regained, meaning that the item will be reduced in quality and value every time it is repaired. Repairing an item with at least 90% durability using this method does not reduce quality.

2) The second way is the most common method players use to repair their durability, without losing any quality at all. You may have seen "alliance" and "corp" repairs pop up in the chat, where a few of your random items are repaired by 10% by other members of your corp/alliance. These repairs cost roughly 1.5 tech points per item being repaired, and will repair either 2 (alliance) or 5 (corp) items for every member online.

To trigger a corp repair (repairs 5 items per player by 10% of every player online in corp), type in chat:

:corp repair mytp confirm

To trigger an alliance repair (repairs 2 items per player by 10% of every player online in alliance), type in chat:

:alliance repair mytp confirm

TP will be automatically subtracted from your wallet. Be aware that if there are many players online, this may cost you a substantial amount of TP. Many players wait until there is almost no one online to spam these repairs on the cheap.

docs.title: Lucky's Arena Notes
docs.text:
By LuckyMFer.

I have a few notes to add:

General rule of thumb - any hull under 10k will be insta targeted and killed. Shields are very helpful but 9/10 players will still shoot a low hull first even if the shield is huge. Hacks are more relevant in arena than anywhere else since they disrupt crux drives, effectively stunning ships upon movement to a new sector.

Most players work their way up to emps for max dps, but this isn't necessarily ideal for arena. Smaller ships get speed/targeting bonuses, strike team bonus, and better utilities for things like hack, grap, hackram. Advanced players prefer natural movespeed which is effected by ship size (a flagship will move at 3.3s whereas destroyer moves at 3.2s). All good things to keep in mind if you're trying to be competitive.

Slow moving emps make prime tgts due to their high damage output, and they're often left behind as fodder by fast drivers when the stack is overpowered.

Strategy-wise, it's most important to think about who to target. Players like MyBankai are able to dominate entire stacks by themselves, so the only way to beat them when you're outmatched is to work with any other remaining teams to take down the juggernaut. It's always tempting to go for the easy kill but this will not win you a match (unless it's chaos arena).

Typically, your driver will set the target for your stack if you are using the assist function (as you should be), but this is an important concept to understand especially if you find yourself driving your stack. If you find yourself in a 3 team battle, don't sit back and wait for two teams to kill each other before engaging. Stay close to the action and time your jumps to move in just after the enemy stacks hit each other.

Again, pick your target intentionally. If one team is winning or overpowered, that should be your target. Use the losing team to beef up your dps by leaving them alone and focusing fire on the biggest threat.

docs.title: Settings Quick Start
docs.text:
By cattabliss.

Should I change any settings? There are a number of settings you should be aware of when you start playing the game that will help improve your experience.

Display local sector ship counts: Gameplay settings. Turn on. Sector counts show how many friendly and enemy ships are in your sector in the top right corner of the screen. This is important for a few reasons: every stack in a single sector can have a max of 75 allied ships, having your sector count on will help you prevent causing collision with friendly players. Another reason is to identify the number of enemy ships in the sector.

Always show sector name text: Gameplay settings. Turn on. This will help you identify the numbers (12/8) of sectors on the main screen. It can help you find locations or bosses for missions.

UTC time/local time: Gameplay settings. The game operates on UTC time, and the game starts daily at 00:00 UTC (the point when Monday turns to Tuesday) and new events are rolled every 8 hours, at 00:00, 08:00. 16:00 UTC.

Camera snap back delay: Gameplay settings. This is the setting that lets you control (in seconds) the time it takes for you to "snap back" to look at your ships, after you have scrolled away to look somewhere else on the map.

Top down/Side View Gameplay settings. This lets you look at your ships from a "2d" perspective versus a 3d perspective.

Show player ships over NPC ships Ship button list. Turn on. Especially important in PVP, this setting lets you see enemy players at the top of the list when they are mixed in with NPC ships.

Sort allied ships most in danger: Ship button list. Turn on. This setting will let you identify which friendly ships are most in danger of dying, it can help keep your team mates alive in any situation where you are taking damage.

Ship Buttons: Ship buttons > show buttons. Set all settings to always with the exception of resources.

docs.title: Specials Quick Start
docs.text:
By cattabliss.

Specials and what do they do Specials are an item slot. Like other items, they are affected by quality and by whether they have "void" status. Unlike other items, specials cannot have their quality repaired/increased through in-game quality kits. Specials generally only realize their full potential when ranked to ultimate. The following is a list of specials in the game, ranked by no particular order:

  • Technician: probably the best special in the game. An ultimate variant of this allows your repairs to heal 1,200 hull points per click, have a chance to crit for 2,400 hull points of heal, plus splash up to four other ships with heal, and also apply a repair-over-time status to any ship affected by its heal. Ships affected by repair-over-time are immune to all status effects for approximately 10 seconds, except for advanced hack 3, which disables abilities.
  • Prospector: the perfect farming special. Normally, harvesting an asteroid or debris grants 1 resource per # size of an asteriod. A roid listed as 12/12 will loot up to 12 resources. A prospector gives a chance to create bonus resources, potentially causing that 12/12 rock to yield 40 resources or more. Pair it with harvesters for max goodness.
  • Advanced Munitions: the best special to make your gun do more damage. It adds critical damage, raw damage, splash chance. Pair it with a high crit ship.
  • Scout: The special that gives you a little bit of speed, and the power to see what is around you. Ultimate scout specials can decloak any ship.
  • Stalker: The opposite of a scout. This special makes you invisible for a short amount of time and can be useful as a method to escape when being targeted or chased. You can only be decloaked if you are scanned by a more powerful scanner, if the cloak timer runs out, or if you fire your weapon.
  • Advanced Propulsion: the special to make you move fast. Most commonly found on carriers.
  • Alien Technology: the most basic of specials. Add +1 EP, not much else. Generally only used by those under lv 10. Otherwise, its usefulness is for deconstruction, to create new special blueprints.
  • Alien Hunter: a special that helps you generate more loot when killing NPCs. This special affects the loot rate modifier, meaning more resources, more frequent and better drops, and is stackable. In general, the loot rate modifier is maxed in the presence of an ulimate alien hunter, or a combination of lower rarity alien hunters.
  • Hacker: Hacker special ranked to ultimate allows you to use hack to disable an enemy ship's abilities roughly once every 35 seconds. An ultimate hack can remove a technician's repair-over-time and stop a crux drive from functioning.
  • Grappling Hook: a hook ranked to ultimate allows you to "drag" an enemy with you into the next sector. It is an effective PVP item used commonly in medium or smaller ships. The drag ability only works at ultimate rarity.
  • Nova Device: a device that makes it so, when your ship dies, you explode after a short delay, damaging all non-friendly ships around you. Nova can be disabled via advanced hack.
  • Deflector: most commonly used in structures, gives a % chance to deflect the damage shot at you into damage onto the person shooting at you.
  • Vampire: drains shields and batteries. Nothing special happens at ultimate. This special is not really used.
  • Battle Ram: one of the best specials for a new player. Creates % chance of a ram, causing large damage, when moving. Ram kills do not reward XP, but rams can be used to lower the health of an NPC ship significantly, allowing you to finish it in a few shots for full XP.
  • Advanced Electronics: a combat special that adds resistances and critical chance. It is a lesser used special as it offers no competitive advantages at ultimate rarity.
  • Advanced Construct: a support special that increases EP, hull points and other soft stats, while granting immunity to ram type attacks.
  • Drone Master: Increases damage and heals done by drones, and reduces ship target speed. Not commonly used.
  • Advanced Shields: Increases shields by a percentage in addition to a stat bonus, plus increases to other defensive resist stats.
  • Tank: Decreases damage output in exchange for increased hull and taunt ability, which forces an enemy ship to target you. Damage output debuff can be nearly negated at 256 void ultimate.

docs.title: Tech Points Quick Start
docs.text:
By cattabliss.

What are tech points (TP)? What should I spend them on? Tech Points (TP) are one of the three basic currencies in The Infinite Black 2. You will acquire TP through in-game achievements, completing missions or purchasing them with credits (1,000 credits = 1 TP) or real life money on tib2.com. TP can be purchased with credits, but cannot be sold or traded. They have a few functions:

  • Augmenting items: increasing an item's rarity. The cost to increase an item's rarity increases with rarity. An uncommon special costs 18 tech points to increase in rarity to rare, a rare costs 36 TP to augment to ultra rare, an ultra rare costs 240 TP to increase to an elite, and so on. In general, only items of at least 230+ quality at uncommon rarity are worth augmenting. To augment an item, click the item and click "upgrade".
  • Ranking up/down items: some items can be increased or decreased in size, including: weapons, shields, storages, armors. Increasing an item's size will generally increase the item's positive and negative stats. The main reason you will want to increase or decrease an item's size is to maximize the use of your ship's slots. To rank an item, click the item and click "upgrade" -- it is in the same menu as augment.
  • Teleporting: as long as you are not flagged in combat, you may teleport to any NPC garrison that you have "studied" and any corp/alliance structure, regardless of whether you have visited the corp/alliance structure. Each teleport costs 1 TP. Teleporting can be accessed in the three line "hamburger" menu in the top left.
  • Repairs: there are two ways to repair items. One way is to click "upgrade" on any item's menu, and hitting repair. However, this first method will reduce the quality of your item. Another way is to use a chat code :corp repair mytp confirm which will repair 5 random equipped items by 10% of every online player in the corporation, at an approximate cost of 1.5 tech points per item.

docs.title: Items
docs.text: Ships and structures equip items to gain stats, weapons, and abilities. Loot drops, crafting, and rewards all provide gear.

docs.title: Item Types
docs.text:
There are 9 equipment types in TIB2:

Type Purpose Notes Weapon Damage output Uses targeting and usually consumes battery. Armor Hull and defense Can be ranked up or down. Shield Shield capacity and resists Can be ranked up or down. Battery Power capacity and recharge Extra battery slots use auxiliary-slot rules. Storage Resource capacity Can be ranked up or down. Drone Fighter, Repair, or Harvester Main drone actions work at full strength in auxiliary slots. See Drones. Engine Movement and special movement Affects travel and movement builds. Computer Targeting and utility Often supports accuracy, lock speed, and ability stats. See Tech Computers for CPU deconstruction upgrades. Special Unique abilities (e.g. Technician, Tank) Often unlocks technology-like effects. See Special Items.

Gear Score is the sum of your equipped items' scores (based on rarity, quality, rank). Many activities (e.g. The Arena) require a minimum Gear Score.

For weapons, drones, and batteries, the first slot gives full item stats while auxiliary slots give reduced stats. See Equipment Slots.

Weapons, armor, shields, and storage have Rank. Changing rank modifies both the item's power and its Equip Point cost. Rarity, quality, rank, and repairs are covered on Item Upgrades. Restrictions for repair, scrap, deconstruction, auction, and corp-bank actions are covered on Item Management.

docs.title: Drones
docs.text: Drones have three roles: Fighter, Repair, and Harvester. See Drones for drone roles, faction differences, action timing, Equip Point cost, and Drone Master notes.

docs.title: Stat Bonus Diminishing Returns
docs.text:
Diminishing returns in The Infinite Black 2 means that the more you stack a single stat, the less each extra point helps. This keeps balance (e.g. no infinite evasion) and lets newer players compete with less maxed gear. The concept applies to many stats: once you have more than a certain amount, additional gear that adds the same stat is slightly less effective. Your ship's displayed stats already reflect the diminished value.

Example: Outgoing Damage. Once you have more than +5% Outgoing Damage, any extra Outgoing Damage from gear is slightly less effective. So if all your items give +6% Outgoing Damage, your ship might show about +5.8% instead of 6%.

docs.title: Resist Stat Table
docs.text:
For resist-type stats (e.g. resist damage), the total from gear is converted to an actual value used in combat as follows:

TOTAL Resist Stat ACTUAL Resist Stat 05 05.00 10 10.00 15 15.00 20 20.00 25 25.00 30 28.85 35 32.41 40 35.71 45 38.79 50 41.67 55 44.35 60 46.88 65 49.24 70 51.47 75 53.57 80 55.56 85 57.43 90 59.21

So stacking 90 total resist gives an actual 59.21; the higher you go, the more each point is scaled down.

docs.title: Chance Stat Table
docs.text:
For chance-type stats (e.g. critical chance, splash chance), the total from gear is converted to an actual value as follows:

TOTAL Chance Stat ACTUAL Chance Stat 05 05.00 10 10.00 15 15.00 20 18.85 25 22.41 30 25.71 35 28.79 40 31.67 45 34.35 50 36.88 55 39.24 60 41.47 65 43.57 70 45.56 75 47.43 80 49.21 85 50.90 90 52.50

So stacking 90 total chance gives an actual 52.50. As with resists, going past 20-25 shows clear diminishing returns.

docs.title: Why It Matters
docs.text:
• Build diversity -- Maximizing one stat has a ceiling; mixing stats and item types is encouraged.
  • Catch-up -- Newer players can close the gap with less perfect gear because heavy stacking is less efficient.
  • Display -- The ship inspect panel shows the actual (diminished) values, not the raw sum from items.

docs.title: Needs data
docs.text: The wiki currently has tables for resist-type and chance-type stat scaling. It does not yet include a complete formula or table for every stat category. Add additional formulas only after they are verified.

docs.title: Drones
docs.text:
Drones are equipment items that perform automatic ship actions. They come in three roles: Fighter, Repair, and Harvester.

Shared target-lock and attack-readiness rules live on Combat and Targeting.

docs.title: Drone Roles
docs.text:
Role Main action Notes Fighter Attacks enemies. Counts with weapons for normal attacks, does not consume battery, and ignores size disparity when targeting. Repair Repairs hull over time. Can benefit from some repair technology, but does not behave exactly like the manual Repair ability. Harvester Harvests asteroids and debris. Used for resource gathering and farming.

For weapons, drones, and batteries, the first slot gives full item stats while auxiliary slots give reduced stats. A drone's main action still works at full strength in an auxiliary slot; only its normal item stats are reduced. See Equipment Slots.

docs.title: Factions and Crafting
docs.text: Drone crafting categories combine faction and role, such as Pirate Fighter Drones, Het Repair Drones, or Precursor Harvest Drones.

docs.title: Fighter Drones
docs.text:
Fighter drones behave like battery-free weapons:

  • A ship needs at least one weapon or fighter drone to make normal attacks.
  • Fighter drones are considered alongside weapons when the game chooses the next attack item.
  • Fighter drones use target-lock timing and their own cooldown timing.
  • Fighter drones ignore size disparity, which helps large ships hit small targets.

Fighter drone attack timing improves with rarity:

Rarity step Attack interval Common 14.0 s Each rarity above Common 0.5 s faster

Exact displayed fighter drone damage depends on rarity, faction, quality, and other modifiers.

docs.title: Repair Drones
docs.text:
Repair drones automatically repair hull:

  • Repair drones still repair at full strength from auxiliary slots.
  • Repair drones with Technician can remove negative effects.
  • Repair drones cannot splash repair or apply repair-over-time.

Repair drone timing improves with rarity:

Rarity step Repair interval Common 26.0 s Each rarity above Common 1.0 s faster

Exact displayed repair amount depends on rarity, faction, quality, and other modifiers.

docs.title: Harvester Drones
docs.text:
Harvester drones gather resources from asteroids and debris. Harvest selection only considers Harvester drones and uses the Harvester with the lowest remaining harvest cooldown.

Base harvest timing improves with rarity:

Rarity step Base harvest interval before faction modifier Common 60.0 s Each rarity above Common 5.0 s faster

Faction changes harvest timing and resource amount:

Faction Harvest interval modifier Resources per harvest action Pirate 100% 2 Het 110% 3 Wyrd 120% 4 Human 130% 2 Precursor 140% 5

Higher resource amount can come with slower harvest timing. Other harvesting bonuses, such as Freighter, Prospector, or Drone Master, may change practical results.

docs.title: Equip Point Cost
docs.text:
Drone Equip Point cost depends on role, faction, and rarity.

Role modifier:

Role Modifier Harvester +0 Repair +1 Fighter +2

Faction and rarity use the role modifier as the starting point:

Faction Common-Uncommon Rare-Ultra-Rare Elite-Legendary Ultimate Pirate 1 + modifier modifier modifier - 1 modifier - 2 Het 2 + modifier 1 + modifier modifier modifier - 1 Wyrd 3 + modifier 2 + modifier 1 + modifier modifier Precursor 4 + modifier 3 + modifier 2 + modifier 1 + modifier Human modifier modifier modifier, except Legendary is modifier - 1 modifier - 2

Drone EP cost cannot go below 0.

docs.title: Drone Master
docs.text:
Drone Master improves drone performance and can add drone-slot utility at high rarity:

Drone Master rarity Attack Drones tech Repair Drones tech Common-Ultra-Rare Rank 1 Rank 1 Elite Rank 2 Rank 2 Legendary Rank 3 Rank 3 Ultimate Rank 4 Rank 4

At Ultimate rarity, Drone Master can also add +1 Drone Equipment Item Slot.

docs.title: Needs Data
docs.text:
• Full displayed damage tables for Fighter drones.
  • Full displayed repair tables for Repair drones.
  • Exact stacking rules between Drone Master, Prospector, Freighter, and other harvesting modifiers.
  • Full drone class/name list by faction and version.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Items
  • Equipment Slots
  • Weapons
  • Combat and Targeting
  • Special Items
  • Open World Farming
  • Size and Targeting

docs.title: Equipment Slots
docs.text: Ships can equip multiple items of some types, such as weapons, drones, and batteries. The first slot and auxiliary slots do not behave the same way.

docs.title: Main slot vs auxiliary slots
docs.text:
For weapons, drones, and batteries:

Slot Item stats Main item action First slot 100% of item stats 100% Auxiliary slots 10% of item stats 100%

Auxiliary slots give only 10% of all stats on that item.

The item's main action still works at full strength:

  • Weapons still shoot at 100%.
  • Fighter drones still attack at 100%.
  • Repair drones still heal at 100%.
  • Harvester drones still harvest at 100%.

Technologies from items apply at full strength even when the item is in an auxiliary slot.

docs.title: What auxiliary scaling affects
docs.text:
Auxiliary scaling affects the item's stat package, not the item type's main action.

Examples:

  • A weapon in an auxiliary slot still fires normally, but its stat bonuses are reduced.
  • A repair drone in an auxiliary slot still repairs normally, but its stat bonuses are reduced.
  • A harvester drone in an auxiliary slot still harvests normally, but its stat bonuses are reduced.
  • A battery in an auxiliary slot still functions as a battery, but its stat bonuses are reduced.

For build advice about which item belongs in which slot, see Build Philosophy or Ship Building Strategy.

docs.title: See also
docs.text:
• Items
  • Drones
  • Stats
  • Equip Points
  • Weapons

docs.title: Gravity Drives
docs.text: Gravity Drives are a crafted engine type with a unique "Instant Jump" ability. They give each move a chance to be free -- the ship arrives in the next sector with no movement cooldown. They also negate movement penalties such as those from the Interdictor Carrier.

docs.title: What Gravity Drives Do
docs.text:
1. Instant Jump chance -- On each sector move, there is a chance the jump is instant: no movement cooldown in the destination sector.
  2. Negate Interdictor -- Interdictor Carriers slow enemy movement and increase targeting time in their sector. Gravity Drive negates these penalties for the wearer, so ships with Gravity Drives can move and fight more normally in Interdictor-affected space.
  3. Fleet following -- When a ship with a Gravity Drive is leading a fleet, any following ship that also has a Gravity Drive equipped will trigger its own instant moves. So Gravity Drive fleets can move together and all benefit from instant jumps when they roll.

docs.title: Leadership (Carriers) and Gravity Drives
docs.text:
Ships with the Leadership ability (e.g. Carriers) use special rules when leading a fleet:

  • They can quick-jump every ship following them with or without Gravity Drives.
  • Their quick-jump speed is slower than the "instant" Gravity Drive jump. The fastest-routing implications belong in Build Philosophy or another strategy page.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Aspect Detail Type Crafted engine Effect Chance for Instant Jump (no movement cooldown next sector) vs Interdictor Negates Interdictor movement/targeting penalties Fleet Followers with Gravity Drives trigger their own instant moves when leader has one Carriers Leadership quick-jumps all followers but slower than Gravity Drive instant

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Interdictor Carrier -- Movement penalty that Gravity Drive negates
  • Item Crafting -- How to craft Gravity Drives
  • Ships -- Ship classes and fleet movement
  • Size and Targeting -- Movement warm-up; Skip Drive and Gravity Drive reduce to 0.5s

docs.title: Item Crafting
docs.text:
Captain Panel -> ITEM CRAFTING -- Blueprints and resources live here. To craft an item, collect the resources for a blueprint and click the blueprint. You discover new blueprints when you Research an item instead of scrapping it.

Crafting is connected to corporations, resources, blueprints, and crafting skills.

docs.title: Crafting requirements
docs.text:
Item crafting checks:

  • The item class must be valid.
  • The blueprint's class and variant bytes must be valid for that item class.
  • The player must have the matching item plan/blueprint unlocked.
  • The craft skill for that item class is used.
  • Resource requirements are calculated per resource type.
  • If the crafting action is enforcing cooldown, any remaining cooldown for that craft skill blocks crafting.
  • Missing resources block crafting.

Some menus may preview requirements without enforcing the cooldown. The exact cooldown display text is Needs confirmation.

docs.title: Quality
docs.text:
Each craft gives crafting skill. Skill improves the quality of crafted items:

  • Quality range: 1 to 255.
  • Each point: +0.1% to the item's stats -> 25.4% difference between worst and best of the same rarity.
  • Outside The Arena, loot with quality above 100 is rare.

docs.title: Skill Categories
docs.text:
There are 6 item crafting skill categories:

Category Builds Scientist Engines and Special-type items. Engineer Computers, Batteries, Storage. Weapons Weapons. Armor Armor. Shields Shields. Drones Drones.

Most Engine and Special-type items enter the game only through crafting or unique events.

docs.title: Crafted item ownership
docs.text: Crafted items are tagged with the creator's name.

docs.title: Commands
docs.text:
• Queue -- Click a blueprint multiple times to queue several crafts.
  • :CRAFT in chat -- Management commands (e.g. clear queue, set lower skill rank for next craft).
  • :CRAFTVOID -- Used by crafters for Void crafting.

docs.text:

docs.title: Item Management
docs.text: Item management covers what can be repaired, modified, scrapped, deconstructed, traded, sold, or moved to shared storage. Use Item Upgrades for rank, rarity, quality, and repair actions; use this page for restrictions that decide whether an item action is allowed.

docs.title: Common Restrictions
docs.text:
Many item actions are blocked when the item is in a special state:

State What it blocks Missing or deleted item The item cannot be modified. Item Lock Blocks scrap, deconstruct, auction, and moving to corporation bank. Listed for sale Cancel the listing before scrapping, deconstructing, or modifying the item. In trade Items currently in trade cannot be modified. Temporary rarity upgrade Temporarily upgraded items cannot be scrapped or deconstructed. Equipped in Arena queue or Arena Equipped items cannot be modified while the owning ship is queued for Arena or inside Arena.

Some actions also require the item to be owned by the player and in the correct location, such as inventory or bank.

docs.title: Repairs
docs.text:
Equipment can lose durability when ships die. At 0% Durability, an item cannot be equipped until repaired.

Repair path Notes Item inspect -> Upgrade -> Repair Repairs the item to full durability, but normal repair can reduce item quality. :corp repair mytp confirm Repairs 5 random equipped items by 10% for every online corporation member and spends Tech Points. :alliance repair mytp confirm Repairs 2 random equipped items by 10% for every online alliance member and spends Tech Points.

Repair is blocked if the item is already at 100% durability or if the item cannot be repaired. On the Oblivion Server, item repair is disabled.

docs.title: Rank and Rarity Actions
docs.text:
Rank Up and Rank Down are limited to:

  • Weapons
  • Armor
  • Storage
  • Shields

Rank Up is blocked when the item's base Equip Point cost is already at the upper limit. Rank Down is blocked when the base Equip Point cost is already at the lower limit.

Rarity upgrades are blocked for Basic items and Ultimate items. On the Oblivion Server, items cannot be upgraded to Ultimate with Tech Points.

For upgrade effects and known cost gaps, see Item Upgrades.

docs.title: Scrap and Deconstruct
docs.text:
Scrapping turns items or resources into Credits. Deconstruction is used for some progression systems, such as Tech Computers and Special blueprints.

Deconstruction is blocked when:

Restriction Notes Crafted item Crafted items cannot be deconstructed. Free starter item Free starter items cannot be deconstructed. No deconstruction value or chance Items with no deconstruction result cannot be deconstructed. Temporary rarity upgrade Temporarily upgraded items cannot be deconstructed. Item Lock, trade, or sale listing Clear the blocking state first.

Some consumables cannot be scrapped. Free starter items cannot be scrapped until the player reaches level 3.

docs.title: Corporation Bank
docs.text:
Moving an item to the corporation bank requires:

  • The player must be in a corporation.
  • The corporation bank must have space.
  • The item cannot be locked.

Corp-bank storage is shared. Treat deposits as a corporation action, not a personal bank extension.

docs.title: Market and Auctions
docs.text:
Auctionable items must be in the player's inventory and cannot be:

  • Free starter items
  • No-drop items
  • Locked items
  • Items currently in trade

Items for sale must be in the player's bank. Items with No Repair cannot be sold at 0% durability.

Buyout requires an active auction with a buyout price. A player cannot buy out their own auction. If the buyer is already the high bidder, the buyout cost is reduced by their current bid; otherwise the full buyout price is due.

docs.title: Free Starter and Bound Items
docs.text:
Free starter items and ships have extra restrictions. Starter items cannot be used in some activities, such as The Arena, and cannot be equipped to defense units or Hardcore units.

Account-bound ships and free starter ships cannot be bought, traded, or sold.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Items
  • Item Upgrades
  • Tech Computers
  • Credits
  • Tech Points
  • Chat Commands

docs.title: Item Reference
docs.text: This page collects item formulas and lookup tables. Use it for stable numbers; use the other item pages for explanations.

docs.title: Quality Multiplier
docs.text:
The quality multiplier is:

quality multiplier = (void item ? 1.1 : 1.0) + qualityValue / 1000

qualityValue is the item's internal quality value.

Quality value range Quality name less than -120 Terrible -120 to -101 VeryLow -100 to -71 Low -70 to -41 Poor -40 to -11 Fair -10 to 9 Average 10 to 39 Good 40 to 69 High 70 to 99 Superior 100 to 119 Perfect 120+ Masterwork

docs.title: Gear Score
docs.text:
Item gear score is:

round(rarity gear-score base * quality multiplier + item EP cost * rarity)
Rarity Gear-score base Common 25 Uncommon 50 Rare 100 Ultra-Rare 200 Elite 300 Legendary 450 Ultimate 700

docs.title: Required Player Level
docs.text:
Base required levels by rarity:

Rarity Base required level Common 0 Uncommon 0 Rare 0 Ultra-Rare 8 Elite 12 Legendary 16 Ultimate 20

Item-family multipliers:

Item family Required level rule Weapon base Armor base Shield base Storage base * 80% Drone base * 115% Engine base * 150% Computer base * 125% Special base * 175% Battery base * 90% Consumable 0

Integer math is used for the family multipliers.

docs.title: Equip Point Cost Notes
docs.text:
Equip Point formula notes:

  • Weapons, armor, and storage use max(0, item rank - item rarity + 1).
  • Shields use the same pattern plus a shield-faction modifier: Human/default 0, Pirate 1, Het 2, Wyrd 3, Precursor 4.
  • Drones use a separate faction/category/rarity table and then clamp negative values to 0.
  • Engines, computers, specials, batteries, and consumables have 0 base Equip Point cost in the checked formulas.

Needs data:

  • Exact rank/base Ep values for weapons, armor, storage, and shields still need a deeper data-table pass before publishing a complete item EP table.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Items
  • Item Upgrades
  • Void Items
  • Equipment Slots
  • Equip Points

docs.title: Item Upgrades
docs.text: The item inspect panel's UPGRADE menu is the canonical place for item rank, rarity, and repair actions. For action restrictions such as item lock, trade, sale listings, deconstruction, auctions, and corporation bank storage, see Item Management.

docs.title: Engineering requirements
docs.text:
Item engineering checks:

  • The item must exist.
  • Items listed for sale must have the sale canceled first.
  • Items currently in trade cannot be modified.
  • Items with a temporary rarity upgrade cannot be modified.
  • Equipped items cannot be modified while the owning entity is in the Arena queue.
  • Equipped items cannot be modified while the owning entity is in Arena.
  • Repair and rank actions are restricted to the player's own equipment.
  • Ranking up an equipped item is blocked if the new Equip Point usage would exceed the entity's total Equip Points.
  • Upgrading an equipped item is blocked if the upgraded item would require a higher entity level than the equipped entity has.
  • Some Void item upgrades require relics in the item crafting bank.

The exact action list and full per-action cost formulas still need extraction from the item-specific upgrade logic.

docs.title: Rarity Augmenting
docs.text:
Increasing an item's rarity is often called augmenting. Higher rarity can add power, unlock stronger item effects, improve some special-item technologies, and sometimes reduce Equip Point pressure.

Known source examples from community guide data:

Upgrade Example cost Uncommon Special -> Rare 18 Tech Points Rare -> Ultra-Rare 36 Tech Points Ultra-Rare -> Elite 240 Tech Points

Needs confirmation

The examples above are guide values, not a complete rarity-cost table. Add more costs only after checking them in game.

Needs data

The wiki does not yet have a complete rarity augment cost table by item type, rarity, or server ruleset.

docs.title: Rank Up and Rank Down
docs.text:
Some item types can be increased or decreased in rank, including:

  • Weapons
  • Shields
  • Storage
  • Armor

Increasing an item's rank generally increases both its positive and negative stats. Rank changes can also change the item's Equip Point cost.

Needs data

The wiki does not yet have the exact rank scaling formula or full Equip Point cost table for ranked item types.

docs.title: Quality
docs.text:
Item quality affects item stats. See Item Crafting for crafting quality and Void Items for Void quality scaling.

Quality thresholds for deciding whether an item is worth upgrading are strategy, not rules. Put that guidance in Build Philosophy or a dedicated item strategy page.

docs.title: Repairs
docs.text:
Equipment can take damage when ships die. At 0% Durability, an item cannot be equipped until repaired.

Known repair paths:

Repair path Notes Item inspect -> Upgrade -> Repair Repairs the item but can reduce item quality. :corp repair mytp confirm Repairs 5 random equipped items by 10% for every online corporation member, at an approximate cost of 1.5 Tech Points per item. :alliance repair mytp confirm Repairs 2 random equipped items by 10% for every online alliance member, at an approximate cost of 1.5 Tech Points per item.

docs.text:

  • Item Management
  • Equipment Slots
  • Equip Points
  • Tech Points
  • Void Items

docs.title: Loot and Reward Reference
docs.text: This page collects loot and reward data that can be documented safely. It does not list drop odds unless a specific formula is known.

docs.title: Use This For
docs.text: If you need to know... Use What cargo reward containers can hold Cargo Reward Entities Standard resource, Skull, or Relic pile sizes Consumable Piles Scavenger or Harvesting bonus text Scavenger and Harvesting Bonus Text Which pity counters exist Pity Counter Fields Party Box task-count caps by rarity Party Box Max Counts Possible daily or event reward descriptions Possible Daily/Event Reward Text

docs.title: Cargo Reward Entities
docs.text:
Cargo rewards can appear in three forms:

Reward type Contains CargoMoney Credits, Skulls CargoItem Item id CargoResource Resource, count

These are reward container types, not drop chances. Use them to understand what a reward object can contain.

docs.title: Consumable Piles
docs.text:
Pile type Counts Resource piles 100, 500, 1000, 5000 Skull piles 10, 100, 500, 1000 Relic piles 1, 5, 10, 50, 100

Resource and Skull piles are grouped as Crafting Resources. Relic piles are faction-specific.

docs.title: Scavenger and Harvesting Bonus Text
docs.text:
The highest active rank from 1 through 7 is used.

Technology family Rank values Scavenger 2%, 4%, 6%, 8%, 10%, 12%, 14% Fleet Resource and Loot Bonus Chance Harvesting 5%, 10%, 15%, 20%, 25%, 30%, 35% Resource Harvest Bonus Chance; also says Gain XP From Asteroids

These are chance bonuses. The exact roll mechanics still need confirmation.

docs.title: Pity Counter Fields
docs.text:
Pity progress is tracked separately for item rarity tiers:

Counter Notes Pity1 through Pity7 One counter per item rarity tier, from Common through Ultimate PityR Separate pity counter, likely relic-related by naming and training context

Exact thresholds, reset rules, Security Rating multipliers, and relic-pity behavior still need confirmation.

docs.title: Party Box Max Counts
docs.text:
For activity rewards, party-box rarity determines the maximum task count shown.

Activity group Default / Common-Rare Ultra-Rare Elite Legendary Ultimate Missions 3 5 7 9 15 Assassin leaders 3 5 10 15 20 Faction hunts: Pirate/Het/Wyrd/Precursor 10 15 25 35 50 Invasion/Planet/Garrison/Shipyard/Outpost/default 1 1 1 1 1

Buff party counts for Critical, Repair, Grapple, Hack, and Harvest need confirmation before being published as exact counts.

docs.title: Possible Daily/Event Reward Text
docs.text:
Possible daily or event reward states include:

  • Mission Rewards Doubled.
  • Arena Awards Bonus Loot and Skulls.
  • Pirate and Alien Defense Units Drop Bonus Loot.
  • Double Skulls for World PvP Kills.
  • Double Rewards for Alliance Defense Kills.
  • Double Loot for Hardcore Ships in PvP and Homeworld Maps.
  • Invasion Bosses Drop Blueprints.
  • Assassin Leaders Can Drop Powerful Item Blueprints.
  • Invasion Bosses Drop Powerful Holiday Gem Loot.
  • Rescue Drifting Crew for Rewards.
  • Alien Leaders and Invasion Bosses Always Drop Void Loot.
  • Double Item Rewards for Invasion and Arena Victory.
  • Bonus Loot and PvP Skulls with Low Item Durability Damage.
  • Special Holiday Event.

These are descriptions of possible daily/event states, not proof that an event is active now.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Pity Loot
  • Items
  • Party Boxes
  • Daily Events

docs.title: Mutate Item Kit
docs.text:
Mutate Item Kits are crafted consumable items used on a single piece of equipment. Using a kit mutates that item, adding a random stat bonus. Each item can only be mutated once in its lifetime; the mutation is permanent and the bonus is always positive.

Mutate bonuses behave like and stack with Void Item bonuses.

docs.title: How Mutate Kits Work
docs.text:
1. Craft a Mutate Item Kit from your Captain Panel (Item Crafting). The kit targets a specific item type (Weapon, Armor, Shield, etc.); that type is randomly selected when the kit is crafted.
  2. Use the kit on a piece of equipment of the matching type. The kit is consumed.
  3. The target item gains a random mutation: one stat is improved by an amount determined by a rarity roll (see table below).
  4. That item may never be mutated again. If you want a different mutation on the same item, you must use a Re-Mutate Item Kit (see below).

Mutate also increases the scrap and deconstruct value of the item by 50%.

docs.title: Mutation Rarity Chances
docs.text:
When you use a Mutate Item Kit, the strength of the mutation is rolled at random:

Rarity Chance Basic 32% Uncommon 30% Rare 20% Ultra-Rare 10% Elite 5% Legendary 2% Ultimate 1%

Each tier of mutation strength is roughly equal to 0.5 Skill Points of benefit. So an Ultimate mutation is like adding about 3.5 extra Skill Points to your ship for that stat.

The stat that is improved is random -- you do not choose which stat gets the bonus.

docs.title: Equip Point Mutations (Very Rare)
docs.text: There is a very rare chance that the mutation will be an Equip Point mutation, adding +1, +2, or +3 Equip Points to the item. These are especially valuable because they increase how much gear you can fit on a ship.

docs.title: Re-Mutate Item Kit
docs.text:
If you already mutated an item and want to try for a better result:

  • Re-Mutate Item Kit -- Available from Reward Point Gifts (3,000 Reward Points).
  • Using it on a previously mutated item re-rolls the mutation.
  • Re-Mutate is guaranteed to improve the mutation rarity (e.g. Rare -> higher tier). The stat chosen is still random.

So: first mutation = craft and use a normal Mutate Item Kit; later improvements = Re-Mutate Item Kit from Reward Points.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Aspect Detail Source Item Crafting; kit type random at craft Use One-time per item; consumed on use Effect Random stat bonus (or rare +EP); always positive Value ~0.5 Skill Points per tier; Ultimate ~ 3.5 Skill Points Re-roll Re-Mutate Item Kit (Reward Point Gifts, 3,000 RP) -- improves rarity, random stat Other +50% scrap/deconstruct value; stacks with Void bonuses

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Item Crafting -- How to craft Mutate Item Kits
  • Void Items -- Similar extra bonuses that stack with mutate
  • Diminishing Returns -- How stacked stats are scaled
  • Reward Point Gifts -- Re-Mutate Item Kit source
  • Equip Points -- What +EP mutations add to

docs.title: Pity Loot Drops
docs.text:
Most item drops use the Pity Loot System: NPC kills add to hidden counters that make future drops more likely until a drop happens. Pity counters exist for item rarities, but the exact live drop formula still needs confirmation.

See Loot and Reward Reference for pity counters and related reward tables.

Community observations describe approximate breakpoints such as 7-8 kills for Uncommon, 33-40 for Rare, and around 300 kills for Ultra-Rare. Treat those as rough field guidance, not exact guaranteed math.

docs.title: Risk and Reward
docs.text:
Pity counters progress faster in more dangerous areas. You won't see much progress toward Ultra-Rare until around Security Rating 3, and you need to go further for Elite, Legendary, and Ultimate drops.

Quality of loot depends on the level of what dropped it, even for pity drops. Kill higher-level NPCs for better quality gear.

docs.title: PvP and High-Security Bonuses
docs.text:
• PvP maps progress pity faster and can produce a guaranteed rare Relic crafting component.
  • Security Rating 8 maps get a 25% loot boost.
  • Security Rating 9 Homeworld raids get double pity loot.

docs.title: What Does NOT Reset Pity
docs.text:
Training notes say these do not reset normal item pity counters:

  • Stealing items
  • Invasion boss loot
  • Killing NPC defense units
  • Party Boxes and other non-standard loot sources

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Items
  • Maps & Security Rating
  • PvP
  • Relics

docs.title: Special Items
docs.text:
Specials are an item type that can add unique abilities, technology lines, or unusual stat packages. Like other items, Specials are affected by quality, Void status, and mutation. Unlike most item types, Special quality may not be repairable upward with normal in-game quality kits.

Many Specials do not show their full behavior until high rarity or Ultimate rarity. This page is a reference index; build advice belongs in Build Philosophy or the cattabliss Specials Quick Start.

docs.title: Dedicated Rules Pages
docs.text:
Use these pages for the mechanics that already have a stable rules page.

Special or tech Main role Rules page Technician Repair upgrades, Purge, critical repair, splash repair, repair-over-time. Technician Technology Tank Unlocks Taunt at Ultra-Rare and above. Taunt Deflector Negates incoming damage and returns it to the attacker. Deflector Hacker Supports Hack and Advanced Hacking technology. Hacking Grappling Hook Supports Grapple and Advanced Grappling technology. Grapple Vampire Supports Drain and Advanced Draining technology. Drain Drone Master Improves drone attack/repair lines and can add drone-slot utility. Drones

docs.title: Farming and Loot Specials
docs.text: Special Stable behavior Needs data Prospector Adds a chance to create bonus resources when harvesting asteroids or debris. Exact chance, scaling, and interaction with Harvester drones and Freighter. Alien Hunter Improves loot-rate modifier for NPC kills and can stack with other loot-rate sources. Exact cap and stacking formula. Bounty Hunter Associated with PvP damage and PvP bonus XP technology lines. Exact stat package and PvP reward interaction.

docs.title: Combat and Utility Specials
docs.text: Special Stable behavior Needs data Scout Adds scanning utility; community notes say Ultimate Scout can decloak ships. Exact scan power, scan cooldown, and decloak comparison. Stalker Cloaks the ship for a limited time. Decloak sources include stronger scan, timer expiry, or firing. Exact duration and scan comparison. Nova Device On ship death, triggers a delayed explosion that damages non-friendly ships nearby. Advanced Hacking III can disable Special item abilities such as Nova. Damage, radius, delay, and size/unique eligibility. Battle Ram Adds a chance to ram while moving. Ram kills do not grant XP. Damage and chance formula.

docs.title: Stat Package Specials
docs.text:
These Specials mainly add stat packages or broad technology lines. Exact stat packages should not be published until verified by rarity.

Special General role Advanced Munitions Offensive stats such as damage, critical damage, and splash chance. Advanced Electronics Combat utility and resistance-style stats. Advanced Propulsion Movement-focused stats; often associated with fast ships. Advanced Construct Support stats such as Equip Points and hull, plus immunity to ram-type attacks. Advanced Shields Shield percentage and defensive resist stats. Alien Technology Basic Special that adds Equip Points and can be deconstructed for Special blueprints. Weird Alien Artifact Artifact-style Special with special restrictions in some contexts, such as Hardcore Arena.

docs.title: Shared Rules and Interactions
docs.text:
• Advanced Hacking III can disable Special item abilities such as Nova Device and Deflector.
  • Auxiliary slot rules apply if a ship has more than one slot of a type. Technologies from auxiliary items apply in full, while normal item stats are reduced according to aux rules.
  • Rarity, rank, quality, Void, and mutation behavior are documented on Item Upgrades, Void Items, and Mutate Item Kit.
  • Item action restrictions such as repair, deconstruction, trade, auction, and item lock are documented on Item Management.

docs.title: Needs Data
docs.text:
• Exact stat packages by rarity for stat-focused Specials.
  • Exact duration, cooldown, damage, and chance formulas for Specials without dedicated rules pages.
  • Confirmed interaction rules for Prospector, Harvester drones, Drone Master, and Freighter.
  • Confirmed live behavior for Weird Alien Artifact restrictions outside Arena.

docs.title: See Also

docs.title: Stats
docs.text: Ships, defense units, and items provide stats that affect combat, movement, and abilities. Stats from items are subject to Diminishing Returns.

docs.title: Stat List
docs.text: Stat Effect Resource Capacity Max resources a ship can carry per resource type. Move Speed Ship movement speed as a percentage of base speed. Hull Repair Amount repaired with outgoing hull repair actions. Outgoing Damage Bonus Overall damage output (weapons, drones, battle ram, etc.). Outgoing Hull Damage Damage dealt to enemy hull. Shield Damage Damage dealt to enemy shields. Excess rolls over to hull. Shield Pierce Percentage of attack damage vs shields that passes to hull. Incoming Damage Amount of damage the unit receives from attacks. Xp Bonus XP earned from NPC and PvP kills (Ship, Player, Corporation XP). Critical Damage Damage dealt when a critical hit occurs. Critical Chance Chance to land a critical hit. Counters Critical Resist. Critical Resist Reduces chance of receiving critical hits. Hit Chance Accuracy; reduces GRAZE chance. Counters Evasion. Benefits hotbar abilities (Grapple, Hack). Evasion Chance Chance to convert incoming attacks into a GRAZE. Counters Hit Chance. Drain Power Strength of the Drain hotbar ability; counters Drain Resist. Drain Resist Counters the Drain hotbar ability. Grapple Power Grapple hotbar ability duration and strength; counters Grapple Resist. Grapple Resist Counters the Grapple hotbar ability. Hack Power Hack hotbar ability duration and strength; counters Hack Resist. Hack Resist Counters the Hack hotbar ability. Splash Chance Chance that attacks deal area-of-effect damage. Counters Splash Resist. Splash Resist Reduces or negates AoE damage. Also benefits vs Nova and Garrison Strike. Power Recharge Battery recharge rate. Power Capacity Max battery capacity. Max Hull Total hull of the unit. Shield Capacity Total shields of the unit. Targeting Speed Speed at which weapons and drones lock onto targets.

docs.title: Tech Computers
docs.text:
Tech Computers are upgraded computer items created through computer deconstruction progress. They have three tiers: Tech I, Tech II, and Tech III. Higher tiers grant stronger positive stats and Equip Point bonuses compared with similar MK computers.

Planning advice belongs in Computer Deconstruction Strategy or the cattabliss Computer Deconstruction Quick Start.

Needs data

The wiki has the unlock cadence and progress logic, but not the full list of Tech Computer stat packages. Add exact stat tables only after checking current in-game values.

docs.title: Deconstruction Progress
docs.text:
Computer deconstruction grants progress toward upgrade kits:

Rule Detail Progress target 1,000 points in a computer category unlocks a kit. Category progress Deconstructing a computer grants points toward that computer's category. Victor progress Deconstructing any CPU also grants equal progress toward the Victor computer category. Points by rarity Common grants 1 point, Uncommon 2, Rare 3, and so on by rarity rank. Progress location Player name -> Crafting -> Blueprint Unlock Progress.

docs.title: Kit Cadence
docs.text:
Unlock count Reward Unlocks 1-4 in a cycle A kit that upgrades a computer from Tech I to Tech II. Every 5th unlock The Tech I to Tech II kit plus a kit that upgrades Tech II to Tech III.

The fifth unlock matters because the extra Tech II to Tech III kit follows the computer category that reaches the milestone.

General restrictions on whether an item can be deconstructed are covered on Item Management.

docs.title: Needs Data
docs.text:
• Full list of computer categories and current names.
  • Exact point values for every rarity above Rare.
  • Exact Tech I, Tech II, and Tech III stat packages.
  • Whether any event, server, or item modifier changes deconstruction progress.

docs.title: Void Items
docs.text: Void items are a rare, powerful variant of regular equipment. Each has an extra stat bonus (similar to mutated items) that grows stronger as you upgrade the item's rarity. Void gear also gains more stats from item quality, making it better than equivalent non-Void gear.

docs.title: How to Get Void Items
docs.text: Source Chance / Notes Crafted items 1:50 chance to be Void. Crafters use :CRAFTVOID in chat. NPC defense units Can drop Void loot. Free Weekly Gift Party Box missions (Captain Panel -> REWARDS -> CLAIM WEEKLY GIFT). Standard Arena Prizes 1:20 chance to be Void. Hardcore Arena Prizes 1:4 chance to be Void. Invasion victory Loot 1:20 chance to be Void. Normal loot 1:5000 chance.

docs.title: Upgrading Void Gear
docs.text:
Void items can be mutated and upgraded like normal gear. To upgrade to the highest rarities (Elite, Mythic, Omega) they cost Relics. The Relic's faction must match the item's faction:

Rarity Relics Elite 1 Mythic 2 Omega 3

docs.title: Re-Void and Conversion
docs.text:
• Re-Void Kits -- From Reward Point Gifts. Let you re-roll the Void bonus randomly.
  • Void Upgrade Kit -- Crafted (Captain Panel -> Item Crafting). Converts a normal non-Void item to Void.

docs.title: Void Rarity Names
docs.text: Normal name Void name Basic Basic Void Uncommon Primal Void Rare Rare Void Ultra-Rare Exotic Void Elite Elite Void Legendary Mythic Void Ultimate Omega Void

docs.title: Weapons
docs.text:
Weapons are one of the nine item types in The Infinite Black 2. They are the primary source of damage for ships and defense units. Weapons, along with Armor, Shields, and Storage, have a Rank that modifies their power and Equip Point cost; you can change rank (and other stats) via the UPGRADE button on the item inspect panel.

Shared targeting, attack-readiness, and hostile-action rules live on Combat and Targeting.

docs.title: How Weapons Work
docs.text:
• Damage -- Weapon damage is modified by stats such as Outgoing Damage Bonus, which affects all damage (weapons, drones, battle ram, etc.). See Stats for the full list.
  • Targeting -- Before a weapon can fire, the ship must acquire a target lock. Ship size affects targeting speed: large ships target small ships more slowly. Targeting Speed stat modifies lock-on speed.
  • Battery -- Most weapons consume battery to fire; Battery and Engine items affect capacity and recharge.
  • Defense units -- Weapons and fighter drones on structures attack faster than their listed speed; slower weapons get a bigger bonus. Defenses also ignore size-based targeting limits and target nearly instantly.
  • Attack requirement -- A ship needs at least one weapon or fighter drone to make normal attacks.

docs.title: Attack Readiness
docs.text:
Weapons and fighter drones both use target-lock timing and their own cooldown timing. When choosing the next attack item, the game compares each ready weapon or fighter drone and uses the candidate with the lowest remaining effective wait.

Disabled weapon slots are ignored. Clearing a target resets weapon and drone lock timers that have not already locked.

docs.title: Weapon Slots and Fleet
docs.text: Ships have multiple weapon slots. Elite Ranks on the Striker Gunboat add more slots (e.g. Elite Rank 3 adds +1 Weapon Slot, Elite Rank 4 adds another). Equip Points limit how many high-rank weapons you can fit; see Equip Points and Stats.

docs.title: Related Item Types
docs.text:
• Fighter Drones -- Behave like weapons that don't consume battery; they ignore size disparity, so large ships can use them effectively against small targets. See Drones.
  • Armor / Shield -- Defensive items; see Stats for Hull, Shield, and resist stats.
  • Item Crafting -- One of the six crafting categories is Weapons; see Item Crafting.

docs.title: Ships
docs.text: Your fleet is the heart of The Infinite Black 2. You command multiple ships, each with its own equipment, level, and role. Combat is balanced around multiple ships. New-player routing belongs in Getting Started.

docs.title: Ship Classes
docs.text:
There are 10 ship types in TIB2:

Size Classes Small Shuttle, Frigate, Assassin Medium Cruiser, Destroyer Large Battleship, Flagship Huge Carrier, Hades Massive Emperor

Each of the 5 factions (Human, Wyrd, Het, Precursor, Pirate) has its own variant of each class -- 50 different ships in total. Ship size controls movement speed, targeting speed, Equip Points, and more.

Carriers become available at level 35 and can lead a fleet using Carrier Leadership. Carrier Leadership requires corporation membership and lets the fleet inherit the Carrier's movement pacing.

docs.title: Fleet Size Requirements
docs.text:
Your fleet must contain a mix of ship sizes to deploy more slots:

Fleet size Requirement 8 ships At least one deployed ship must be Medium or smaller 9 ships At least two deployed ships must be Medium or smaller 10 ships At least two deployed ships must be Medium or smaller

Small ships also count as Medium-or-smaller. See Small Strike Team Bonus for the benefit of fielding small/medium ships.

docs.title: Captain Level to Deploy More Ships
Starting Ship
docs.text: Players begin in a Striker Gunboat. Striker Gunboats gain more equipment slots and Equip Points with Elite Ranks. You can own 100+ ships of any type and deploy several at once.

docs.title: Carrier Leadership
docs.text:
Carrier Leadership lets a Carrier act as the movement anchor for a fleet. Once a player can use a Carrier, the fleet becomes much easier to move and manage.

Corporation required

Carrier Leadership only works while the player is in a corporation.

docs.title: When carriers unlock
docs.text:
Players can use a Carrier at level 35.

For many pilots, this is one of the first major comfort milestones: the Carrier can be built for speed while slower damage ships follow and inherit the Carrier's movement pacing.

docs.title: Fleet movement
docs.text:
When a Carrier leads a fleet, the rest of the fleet inherits the Carrier's movement pacing.

Carrier Leadership is a rule page. Build advice belongs in Build Philosophy or another strategy page.

docs.title: Move speed limits
docs.text:
Ship size sets a hard minimum move time. Extra move speed does not make a ship move below its size minimum, but it can help counter enemy Interdictor penalties.

See Size and Targeting for the canonical size table.

docs.title: Pre-jump animation
docs.text: Each ship also has a pre-jump animation that increases with size. This is separate from the move-speed cap. See Size and Targeting for warm-up times by size.
