docs.title: Deception Module
docs.text:
The Deception Module is a ship upgrade that hides information from other players -- such as equipped gear, Equip Points used, and various stats. It does not replace or conflict with Ship Mods and works on any ship. You can toggle deception on or off from the Ship Inspect panel.

Deception effects grow stronger with Elite Rank: higher rank hides more slots and item types.

docs.title: Where to Get Deception Modules
docs.text:
• The Arena
  • Holiday Events
  • Reward Point Gifts -- 1,000 Reward Points

docs.title: What Is Hidden (by Elite Rank)
docs.text: More is hidden as the ship's Elite Rank increases. The following lists show what is hidden at or above each rank.

docs.title: Hidden at Elite Rank 0
docs.text:
• Gear Score
  • Cloak / Scan Power
  • Advanced Stats & Resists
  • Equip Points Used
  • Weapon, Drone and Battery Slot 6
  • Storage Item

docs.title: Hidden at Elite Rank 1
docs.text:
• Weapon, Drone and Battery Slot 5
  • Shield Item

docs.title: Hidden at Elite Rank 2
docs.text:
• Weapon, Drone and Battery Slot 4
  • Armor Item

docs.title: Hidden at Elite Rank 3
docs.text:
• Weapon, Drone and Battery Slot 3
  • Computer Item

docs.title: Hidden at Elite Rank 4
docs.text:
• Weapon, Drone and Battery Slot 2
  • Special Item

docs.title: Hidden at Elite Rank 5
docs.text:
• Weapon, Drone and Battery Slot 1
  • Engine Item

So at Elite Rank 5 with Deception on, other players cannot see your gear score, resists, equip points used, or any of your six weapon/drone/battery slots, storage, shield, armor, computer, special, or engine.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Aspect Detail Effect Hides gear, EP used, stats, resists from other players Compatibility Works with all Ship Mods; any ship Toggle Ship Inspect panel Scaling More hidden at higher Elite Rank (0-5) Sources Arena, Holiday Events, Reward Point Gifts (1,000 RP)

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Ship Mods -- Separate from Deception; both can be used
  • Elite Ranks -- Determines how much is hidden
  • Reward Point Gifts -- 1,000 RP for Deception Module
  • The Arena -- Source for Deception Module

docs.title: Elite Ranks
docs.text: Ships and defense units can be upgraded with Elite Ranks using Skulls (earned from PvP). Each rank adds technology, skill points, and/or Equip Points. Use the ELITE RANK button on the Unit Inspect Panel; you can preview improvements without confirming.

docs.title: Ship Elite Rank Costs (Skulls)
docs.text:
Rank Cost 1 100 2 500 3 1,000 4 2,000 5 3,000

Defense unit Elite Rank costs are 5x the ship costs.

docs.title: Striker Gunboat Progression
docs.text:
The starting Striker Gunboat uses special Elite Rank progression. Each Elite Rank adds +3 Equip Points, technology, and extra slots:

Rank Adds 1 +1 Battery Slot 2 +1 Battery Slot 3 +1 Battery Slot, +1 Weapon Slot 4 +1 Drone Slot, +1 Weapon Slot, Reverse Hacking tech 5 +1 Drone Slot, Reverse Grapple tech

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Skulls -- How to earn them
  • Ships
  • Equip Points -- Full Equip Point formula
  • Structures -- Defense unit costs

docs.title: Equip Points
docs.text:
Equip Points are the capacity your ship or defense structure has for mounting weapons, armor, shields, batteries, drones, and other equipment. Larger ships and defense units have more Equip Points.

The sum of all equipped item costs cannot exceed the unit's maximum Equip Points.

docs.title: What changes Equip Point cost
docs.text:
Most equipment has an Equip Point cost. That cost can change when the item is upgraded:

  • Changing the rank of weapons, armor, storage, or shields changes both the item's power and its Equip Point cost. See Item Upgrades.
  • Increasing an item's rarity can reduce its requirements.
  • Some rare bonuses and ship upgrades can add extra Equip Points.

docs.title: Ship Equip Point formula
docs.text:
Ship Equip Points are calculated from several sources:

Source Effect Ship class and faction Provides the base Equip Point value. Hardcore ship Adds +3 Equip Points. Elite rank, non-Striker ships Adds +1 Equip Point per Elite Rank. Elite rank, Striker Gunboat Uses a special formula: +2 plus +3 per Elite Rank. Extra Striker flag Adds +3 more Equip Points. Public trigger still needs confirmation. Ship mod bonus Adds any bonus Equip Points from ship mods. EquipPointMax bonus stat Adds the bonus-stat value to total Equip Points.

Some special units can have 50 Equip Points. Do not use that as a normal ship rule unless the specific unit is confirmed.

docs.title: Crafted ship bonuses
docs.text: There is a 1% chance that a crafted ship gains +1 Equip Point. In very rare cases, that crafted bonus can be +2 or +3.

docs.title: Needs data
docs.text: The wiki does not currently include a full Equip Point table by ship class, faction, defense-unit type, item rank, or item rarity. Add those values only after they are verified.

docs.title: See also
docs.text:
• Ships
  • Ship Crafting
  • Elite Ranks
  • Mutate Item Kit

docs.title: Hardcore Ships & Defense Units
docs.text: Any ship or defense unit can be set to Hardcore (opt-in). Hardcore units gain strong bonuses but drop equipment and lose more durability on death. Corporate Defender ships are never chosen from Hardcore ships -- only non-Hardcore eligible ships can be used as Corp Defenders.

docs.title: Hardcore Bonuses
docs.text:
When a ship or defense unit is Hardcore:

  • -10% Movement Cooldown -- Faster sector-to-sector movement.
  • +25 Skill Points -- Extra skill points to spend.
  • +3 Equip Points -- Ships gain 3 more EP. Corporate defense units gain +10 Equip Points when Hardcore.
  • +5 additional Skill Points -- Hardcore units can invest 5 more skill points (on top of the +25), for greater build flexibility.

docs.title: Hardcore Penalties and Rules
docs.text:
• Drop on death -- When a Hardcore unit dies, it drops 1 random equipped item.
  • No free newbie gear -- Hardcore units cannot equip free starter/newbie gear.
  • Durability -- Hardcore units lose slightly more durability on death than non-Hardcore.

So Hardcore is high risk, high reward: better stats and EP, but death costs you an item and more durability.

docs.title: Corporate Defenders and Phoenix
docs.text:
• Corp Defenders -- Corporate Defender Ships are drawn from corp/alliance members' ships. Hardcore ships are never used as Corp Defenders; only eligible non-Hardcore ships are considered.
  • Phoenix Ship Mod -- Ships with the Phoenix mod cannot go Hardcore, and Hardcore ships cannot use the Phoenix mod.

docs.title: Oblivion Server
docs.text:
On the Oblivion Server, rules are harsher:

  • Hardcore ships lose 100% durability on death and drop all equipped items.
  • Hardcore ships that get the killing shot generate pity loot at 2x the Oblivion accelerated rate, stacking with other loot bonuses (e.g. up to 10x or more during invasions).

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Aspect Detail Bonuses -10% move cooldown, +25 Skill Points, +3 EP (ships) or +10 EP (defenses), +5 extra investable Skill Points Death Drops 1 random equipped item; more durability loss Restrictions No newbie gear; no Corp Defender use; no Phoenix mod Oblivion 100% durability loss and drop all items on death; 2x pity loot on killing shot

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Ship Mods -- Phoenix incompatible with Hardcore
  • Corporate Defenders -- Hardcore ships never used
  • Oblivion Server -- Hardcore death and loot rules
  • Equip Points -- +3 EP (ships) / +10 EP (defenses)
  • Skills -- +25 and +5 extra Skill Points

docs.title: Interdictor Carrier
docs.text:
The Interdictor is a Ship Mod that is crafted from your captain's Item Crafting panel. It allows a Carrier-class ship to become an Interdictor, permanently replacing the Carrier's Leadership bonus with a sector-wide penalty to enemies.

Gravity Drives negate the Interdictor's movement penalties for ships that have them equipped.

docs.title: What the Interdictor Does
docs.text:
• Sector penalty -- The Interdictor projects an effect in nearby sectors that:
  • Increases enemy targeting and movement times by 15% / 20% / 25% / 30% by Interdictor rank.
  • Removes other movement benefits such as Space Lanes.
  • One active source per sector -- Only one Interdictor source can apply the active penalty in a sector at a time. Other Interdictors in the affected sector do not stack the penalty or share control.
  • No effect in The Arena -- The Interdictor effect does not work in The Arena.
Interdictor rank Movement and targeting penalty I +15% II +20% III +25% IV +30%

docs.title: Map Indicators
docs.text:
Interdictor influence is visible in the game world:

  • Enemy Interdictor influence appears as a bright red, pulsing sector indicator. Your ships are penalized in that sector.
  • Friendly Interdictor influence appears as a light gray pulsing ring. Enemies are penalized there.

docs.title: Crafting
docs.text:
• Interdictor is crafted like the Ultimate ship mod.
  • Requires identical fragments and Relics all from the same faction.
  • The final crafted item is the same regardless of faction; only the source materials must match.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Aspect Detail Base ship Carrier only Replaces Leadership bonus Effect +15% to +30% enemy targeting & movement in sector; removes Space Lanes Limit One active Interdictor source per sector Arena No effect Counter Gravity Drives negate the penalty for the wearer Formula status Rank scaling confirmed

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Ship Mods -- All 8 mods including Interdictor
  • Gravity Drives -- Negates Interdictor penalty
  • Elite Ranks -- Stronger effect at higher rank
  • Ships -- Carrier class and fleet roles

docs.title: Retro Ship Skins
docs.text:
Retro Ship Skins are one-time-use items that change the visual model of a ship to an alternate "retro" style. Every ship class has 2 or 3 alternate models that can be discovered or awarded. Skins can be traded or sold. Once used, the skin is consumed and that ship keeps the new look.

A visual reference for skins is available at: https://files.spellbook.com/tib/retroshipskins.jpg

docs.title: Sources of Retro Skins
docs.text: Source Details Holiday Events 6 skins from Holiday Events (gem drops on holiday days). Steam Scorpion skin is a Steam exclusive. Exploring the Universe Retro Hades #3 is a reward for completing the "Exploring the Universe" objective. Reward Point Gifts Six rare skins available via Reward Point Gifts: Retro Carrier Skin #1 and #2, Retro Assassin Skin #1 and #2, Retro Emperor Skin #2, Retro Frigate Skin #2. Other Some skins are not yet available in game.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Aspect Detail Effect Changes ship appearance to a retro model; one-time use per skin Per class 2-3 alternate models per ship class Economy Tradeable, sellable Main sources Holiday Events (6), Steam (Scorpion), Exploration (Retro Hades #3), Reward Points (6 rare)

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Ships -- Ship classes and fleet
  • Holiday Events -- Holiday gem skins
  • Reward Point Gifts -- Rare retro skins
  • Ship Mods -- Gameplay mods (separate from cosmetic skins)

docs.title: Ship Crafting
docs.text: Corporations level up their Shipyards to unlock ship blueprints. Members build ships at the corp's shipyard; each Shipyard is dedicated to one faction (Pirate, Het, Wyrd, or Precursor). Corp leadership chooses the faction, opening advanced alien-class ships with different technology -- more powerful but more expensive.

docs.title: Requirements
docs.text:
Ship construction checks:

  • Ship class and faction must be valid.
  • Some ships/factions require a minimum number of days in a corporation.
  • Some ships/factions require Skulls.
  • Some ships/factions require Relics.
  • Pirate construction uses Human relics for the relic-bank check.
  • The player's ship garage must have room.
  • Resource requirements are calculated per resource type.
  • The acting ship must pass the shared ship validator.
  • Construction requires an eligible friendly defense unit in the current sector, or an eligible corporation-owned Shipyard.
  • Non-human Shipyards are ignored unless owned by the player's corporation.
  • Missing resources block construction.

Larger ships generally have more Equip Points. Exact ship values belong in Ship Reference, not repeated across guide pages.

docs.title: Ship Stat Bonus Mod
docs.text:
When you craft a ship it gains a random Ship Stat Bonus Mod. Its strength is comparable to a number of Skill Points:

  • Random roll: Between 3 and 12 Skill Points equivalent.
  • Maximum (12) is only possible during rare daily crafting events.
  • Ship Stat Modification items can drop as loot, letting you replace this crafted bonus.

docs.title: Corporation Ship Crafting Skill
docs.text: Building a ship increases your corporation's ship crafting skill for that faction (e.g. building a Wyrd Reaper increases Wyrd Ship Crafting Skill). Corporations can earn up to 1,000 ship crafting skill points per day. Higher corp ship crafting skill improves the Ship Stat Bonus Mod on newly built ships.

docs.title: Equip Point Bonus
docs.text: There is a 1% chance a crafted ship gains +1 Equip Point; rarely +2 or +3.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Ships
  • Shipyards
  • Skulls and Relics
  • Corporations
  • Item Crafting

docs.title: Ship Mods
docs.text:
Ship Mods are consumable items that permanently modify a ship. There are 8 of them. Once applied, the ship gains new bonuses and technologies that can be improved further through Elite Ranks. To preview how a ship would change, use the mod on a ship but cancel the final confirmation popup.

Except for Interdictor (Carrier-only), ship mods can be applied to any ship. Although they are effectively permanent, they can be reclaimed with Tech Points from the ship's detail panel.

docs.title: The 8 Ship Mods
docs.text: Mod Effect Main source / notes Medic Adds Repair Speed and Technician technology. Various. Gladiator Adds Vorpal and Savior technology. Guarantees critical attacks vs injured enemies and critical repairs on injured friends. The Arena: earn a Gladiator mod and titles for every 100 victories. Phoenix Adds Rebirth and Savior technology. Converts fatal damage into a large repair and instant cloak; improves repairs on injured friends. Rebirth works in PvP and PvE with separate cooldowns. Invasion NPCs: kill invasion enemies to earn Phoenix mod and titles. Cannot be used on Hardcore ships; ships with Phoenix cannot go Hardcore. Raider Adds bonus damage vs Players and Defense Units. Various. Interdictor Converts a Carrier into an Interdictor; replaces Leadership with sector penalty to enemies. Only one Interdictor can have effect per sector. Crafted (fragments + Relics, same faction). See Interdictor Carrier. Freighter Adds large resource harvesting bonuses but reduces damage. Various; e.g. daily event "Ancient Alien Secrets" -- Freighter mod improves harvesting. Exact doubling behavior needs confirmation. Paladin Adds max hull and defensive technology. Crafted: created only when any Emperor-class ship is crafted as an additional bonus result. Ultimate Adds +4 Equip Points and unlocks additional gear slots. Crafted from "fragments" dropped by NPC defense units or harvested from Relic Shrines. You may only have one deployed Ultimate ship at any time.

docs.title: Crafting Ultimate and Interdictor
docs.text:
• Both Ultimate and Interdictor ship mods are crafted.
  • Crafting requires identical fragments and Relics all from the same faction.
  • There is no difference in the final crafted mod between factions -- only the source of materials must match.

docs.title: Deception Module (Separate Upgrade)
docs.text: The Deception Module is a separate ship upgrade that hides equipped gear, resists, and other stats from other players. It does not replace or conflict with any of the 8 ship mods above and works on any ship.

docs.title: Removing ship modifications
docs.text:
Ship modification removal rules:

  • The ship must be owned and valid for modification.
  • The ship must be recalled before removing a mod.
  • Retro-skin removal requires the current mod to be a retro skin.
  • Technology-mod removal is blocked for unique ships.
  • Technology-mod removal requires the current ship mod to be a technology mod.
  • Bonus-stat removal requires the ship to have a bonus stat.
  • Hardcore removal requires the ship to be hardcore.
  • Removal costs Tech Points.

Exact Tech Point removal costs still need confirmation.

docs.title: Summary
docs.text: Mod Key benefit Limitation / source Medic Repair + Technician Any ship Gladiator Vorpal, Savior; crits vs injured Arena (100 wins) Phoenix Rebirth (save from death), Savior Invasions; no Hardcore Raider Bonus damage vs players & defenses Any ship Interdictor Sector slow for enemies; one per sector Carrier only; crafted Freighter Harvesting bonus, less damage Any ship Paladin Max hull, defensive tech Emperor craft bonus only Ultimate +4 EP, extra slots; one deployed Crafted (fragments + Relics)

docs.title: Ship and Defense Unit Size
docs.text:
Every combat entity has a size: Small, Medium, Large, Huge, or Massive. Size affects movement speed, evasion, and weapon targeting speed. See the Ships page for fleet size and captain level requirements.

Shared combat rules such as target locks, attack readiness, and hostile ability gates live on Combat and Targeting.

docs.title: Targeting
docs.text:
Before weapons can fire, a target lock must be acquired. The greater the size difference, the longer it takes for a large ship to lock onto a small one. Big ships are meant to fight other bigs and have trouble targeting shuttles and frigates.

  • Small ships -- Can't carry as much, but are much more evasive. Use small ships to target other small ships and leave big guns for big targets.
  • Defense units (Planets, Garrisons, etc.) -- No targeting limit; they target nearly instantly. Very slow weapons on defenses get an attack speed bonus.
  • Fighter drones -- Ignore size disparity, so Carriers are strong against small targets.

Targeting speed bonuses reduce lock time, but lock time cannot go below the game's minimum lock-time floor.

docs.title: Movement Warm-Up
docs.text:
Ship size sets pre-move "warm up" time (the pre-jump animation):

Size Warm-up Small 0.7 s Medium 1.0 s Large 1.3 s Huge / Massive 1.7 s

The Skip Drive Engine and Gravity Drives (crafted engine) reduce this to 0.5 s for any ship.

docs.title: Minimum Move Times
docs.text:
Ships also have hard minimum move times by size:

Size Minimum move time Small 3.0 s Medium 3.2 s Large 3.3 s Huge / Massive 3.5 s

These minimums correspond to about -65% move speed for each size. Extra move speed above the minimum can still matter because it helps counter enemy Interdictor movement penalties.

See Carrier Leadership for how Carrier-led fleets use move speed.

docs.title: Build Philosophy
docs.text: Build advice changes with balance patches, corp support, available gear, and PvP habits. Treat this page as community guidance, not game rules. For mechanics, link back to reference pages instead of repeating formulas here.

docs.title: Reference First
docs.text:
Before choosing a build, check the mechanics pages:

  • Equip Points
  • Equipment Slots
  • Stats
  • Diminishing Returns
  • Special Items
  • Ship Mods
  • Carrier Leadership
  • Ship Building Strategy

docs.text: Role Typical focus Useful references Carrier support Movement, fleet bonus uptime, survivability, corp positioning. Carrier Builds, Carrier Leadership, Size and Targeting. docs.title: Notes From Current Sources
docs.text:
• Wyrd shield-heavy farming fleets are repeatedly described as strong, but exact shield and capacitor formulas still need confirmation.
  • Drone Master and Prospector both appear in farming notes, but their exact interaction with Harvester drones and Freighter needs data.
  • Advanced Propulsion appears often in carrier movement discussions. Use movement caps and Interdictor pressure rules as the reference baseline.
  • Meta build labels should be dated or source-labeled when added.

docs.title: Carrier Builds
docs.text:
Carrier build advice changes with enemy Interdictors, available engines, corporation support, and fleet habits. Treat this page as community guidance, not game rules.

For stable mechanics, see Carrier Leadership, Size and Targeting, Interdictor Carrier, and Gravity Drives.

docs.title: What Carriers Do
docs.text:
Carriers can anchor fleet movement through Carrier Leadership while the player is in a corporation. That makes them important for moving slower ships together.

Carrier Leadership does not remove the need to understand movement caps, warm-up times, following range, or Interdictor penalties.

docs.title: Common Build Goals
docs.text:
Carrier builds often balance:

  • Movement speed and movement penalty resistance.
  • Enough durability to stay alive while acting as the fleet anchor.
  • Equipment choices that support the fleet's purpose.
  • Gravity Drive or other movement tools when the build is focused on travel.

Exact item choices are community/meta guidance. Keep item stat formulas on Item Reference and Stats.

docs.title: Interdictor Pressure
docs.text:
Enemy Interdictors increase movement and targeting time in affected sectors. Extra movement speed can still matter even when a ship is at its size-based minimum move time because penalties can push movement back above that minimum.

Gravity Drives negate Interdictor movement penalties for equipped ships.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Carrier Leadership
  • Fleet PvP Strategy
  • Ship Building Strategy
  • Gravity Drives

docs.title: Computer Deconstruction Strategy
docs.text:
Computer deconstruction strategy is about timing unlocks. For the reference rules, see Tech Computers.

Every deconstructed CPU advances its own computer category and also advances Victor. Because of that shared Victor progress, deconstructing every computer you find can cause Victor to reach important unlock milestones before the computer category you actually wanted.

docs.title: Common Planning Pattern
docs.text:
The cattabliss guide suggests a planning pattern:

  • Push the desired computer category close to 1,000 points.
  • Stop roughly 20 to 50 points short.
  • Use other computers to finish earlier unlocks in the cycle.
  • Finish the desired category on the fifth unlock so the bonus Tech II to Tech III kit lands on the computer type you wanted.

Check progress regularly in the player stats menu and in player name -> Crafting -> Blueprint Unlock Progress.

docs.title: Caveats
docs.text:
• This is community planning guidance, not a game rule.
  • The exact best category depends on build goals, current market values, and available computers.
  • The current wiki still needs exact Tech Computer stat tables.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Tech Computers

  • Item Crafting

docs.title: Faction Ship Notes
docs.text:
Faction ship advice changes with shipyard access, corp crafting skill, market supply, and balance patches. Treat this page as community guidance unless a linked reference page says otherwise.

For faction lore and stable map/homeworld notes, see Factions. For shipyard rules, see Shipyards and Ship Crafting.

docs.title: Current Source Notes
docs.text: Faction Practical notes from current sources Status Wyrd Gootchat repeatedly points to Wyrd shield/capacitor behavior and shield-heavy farming. Notes include shield regeneration scaling with max shield and a Wyrd E3-like soak/negation behavior while shields are up. Needs confirmation before exact formulas are added to reference pages. Het Training and chat notes describe Hets as dangerous and useful for early PvP/Arena Cruiser paths. Needs a verified ship-by-ship technology table. Precursor Training and chat notes also point to Precursor Cruisers as early PvP/Arena options. Needs a verified ship-by-ship technology table. Pirate Pirate ships share the alien/faction shipyard path, but current pass has mostly lore and mission references rather than build-specific data. Needs practical ship data.

docs.title: Wyrd Shield Notes
docs.text:
Gootchat has several useful Wyrd shield observations, but they should remain strategy/source notes until verified:

  • Shield-heavy Wyrd ships are repeatedly recommended for farming durability.
  • One source note says Wyrd shield capacitors are "at and above 10% of max."
  • Another note describes an E3 Wyrd behavior where, while the ship has shields, a shot has a 10% chance to be completely soaked/negated.
  • Large shield pools appear to increase periodic shield restoration in observed examples.

These are strong leads for testing, not final formulas.

docs.title: Data Needed
docs.text:
• Ship-by-ship faction technology tables.
  • Exact Wyrd shield capacitor and soak/negation mechanics.
  • Which faction hulls are recommended because of stable mechanics versus current community meta.
  • Whether faction scan appearance or "wavelength" has any gameplay rule beyond visual identity.

docs.title: Fleet PvP Strategy
docs.text:
Fleet PvP strategy changes with players, builds, and balance patches. Treat this page as community guidance, not game rules.

For stable mechanics, see Fleet PvP Rules, Sectors, Skulls, and Interdictor Carrier.

docs.title: Stack habits
docs.text:
Common fleet expectations from training data and gootchat:

  • Do not join a full stack if it would push the sector over the alliance limit.
  • If collision starts, move out or swap stacks when called.
  • Drivers and copilots may split ships across multiple stacks to keep the fight active without wiping the fleet to collision damage.
  • Keep Display local sector ship counts enabled before joining large fleet activity.

docs.title: Sort calls
docs.text:
Commanders call enemy sort orders so allies see targets in the same order and focus fire more reliably.

Common examples from training data and gootchat:

Sort call Meaning Smallest hull first Focus low-hull ships first. Largest hull first Focus high-hull ships first. Smallest size first Focus smaller ships first. Largest size first Focus larger ships first. Most in danger Focus ships already close to death. Worst gear Focus weaker gear-score targets. First to arrive Focus ships by arrival/order list.

Sort calls are operational instructions, not universal strategy. Follow the live fleet call unless your commander tells you otherwise.

docs.title: Driver survival
docs.text:
Gootchat repeatedly emphasizes that driver ships should avoid being at the top of common enemy sort lists. Practical driver-building ideas from those messages include:

  • Blend into common enemy sorts instead of standing out as the easiest target.
  • Avoid being the lowest hull, worst gear, or most obvious high-value target.
  • Consider strong grapple resist, critical resist, stealth tools, and enough survivability to survive focused pressure.
  • Use copilots when possible: one player can drive while another watches collision, sort calls, refill links, and questions.

docs.title: Interdictors and movement pressure
docs.text: Interdictors can slow enemy movement and targeting in affected sectors. Move-speed overstacking can help counter enemy Interdictor penalties, while Gravity Drives negate the movement penalty for ships that equip them.

docs.title: Mission Strategy
docs.text:
Mission strategy changes with player level, map access, corporation activity, and daily objectives. Treat this page as community guidance, not game rules.

For stable mission rules, see Missions and Outpost Missions.

docs.title: Community notes
docs.text:
• For personal exploration missions, some players leave one sector unexplored in a new map until an "Explore System" mission appears.
  • If you will not complete specific personal missions, refreshing up to your daily new-mission limit can help find broader objectives.
  • For corporation/alliance missions, avoid keeping extremely specific objectives if the group cannot realistically complete them.
  • For universe missions, you cannot control the mission; contribute at least once if you want participation credit.

docs.title: Outpost mission notes
docs.text: Community chat describes outpost mission chains as useful for Tech Points and repair support. Exact outpost chain rewards should stay out of reference pages until verified in game.

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Missions
  • Outpost Missions
  • Tech Points

docs.title: NPC Bust Strategy
docs.text:
NPC bust strategy changes with target type, map risk, caller habits, and current player strength. Treat this page as community guidance, not game rules.

For stable PvE references, see NPC Busts, Structures, and Missions.

docs.title: Group-call patterns
docs.text:
Community notes describe calls such as "planet", "uni", "structure", or "clear" busts. A caller may link:

  • A location or structure target.
  • A driver ship to follow.
  • A minimum survivability expectation.

Use Common Workflows for assist/follow basics and Fleet PvP Rules for driver concepts that also apply to large PvE groups.

docs.title: Cautions
docs.text:
• Do not treat old scrap values as fixed prices.
  • Bring enough durability and repair capacity for the target.
  • Check whether the target is in PvE space or PvP space before traveling.
  • If the bust is in PvP space, normal PvP risks apply unless a daily event disables open-world PvP.

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• NPC Busts
  • Open World Farming Strategy
  • Structures

docs.title: New Player Path
docs.text:
This is a practical route for early progression. Treat it as guidance, not a required path.

For stable mechanics and click paths, see How To Play, Common Workflows, FAQ, and Recommended Settings.

docs.title: Early Priorities
docs.text:
1. Follow the first mission and explore Sol.
  2. Reach 100% Sol exploration to earn another ship.
  3. Deploy the second ship.
  4. Join an active corporation.
  5. Turn on useful settings like sector ship counts and sector names.
  6. Learn daily gifts and basic repair workflows.

docs.title: Why Corporation Comes Early
docs.text:
Corporations provide access to:

  • Shared technology and skills.
  • Shipyards and ship construction.
  • Group PvE and PvP calls.
  • Structure defense and alliance activity.
  • Experienced players who can sanity-check expensive choices.

docs.title: When To Branch Out
docs.text: Goal Where to go More credits Credits Quick Start More Tech Points Tech Points Quick Start First Arena attempts Arena Quick Start Ship choices Ship Building Strategy Farming Open World Farming Strategy

docs.title: Open World Farming Strategy
docs.text:
Open-world farming strategy changes with ship builds, map access, corporation support, market demand, and balance patches. Treat this page as community guidance, not game rules.

For stable farming rules and references, see Open World Farming, Pity Loot, and Security Rating.

docs.title: Build guidance
docs.text:
• Use enough survivability for the map.
  • Add harvesting tools if income is the goal.
  • Use Carrier Leadership once available to move slower farming ships more comfortably.
  • Wyrd shield-heavy fleets are repeatedly described in gootchat as strong for farming, but exact shield/capacitor formulas belong on a verified build page.

docs.title: Economy guidance
docs.text: Market values change. Avoid treating old Discord prices as live prices. Ask active players or your corporation, and compare current market listings before deciding what to sell, scrap, or keep.

docs.title: Related
docs.text:
• Open World Farming
  • Credits
  • Wyrd

docs.title: PvP Basics
docs.text:
PvP advice changes with player habits, fleet size, daily events, and balance patches. Treat this page as community guidance, not game rules.

For stable rules, see Player vs Player, Arena, Fleet PvP Rules, and Structure Warfare Rules.

docs.title: Before Entering PvP Space
docs.text:
Check:

  • Whether open-world PvP is active today.
  • Whether your route passes through enemy-controlled maps.
  • Whether your ship durability and item durability are acceptable.
  • Whether you are following a fleet driver or traveling alone.
  • Whether you understand the local sector ship counts.

docs.title: Common PvP Habits
docs.text:
• Join an active corporation and follow alliance calls when possible.
  • Turn on sector ship counts before large fights.
  • Learn assist leader and follow workflows before the fight starts.
  • Keep structure warfare, Arena, and open-world hunting separated in your head; they have different risks and reward rules.

docs.text: Topic Guide Arena entry Arena Quick Start Fleet movement and sort calls Fleet PvP Strategy Structure fights Structure Busting Strategy Build planning Ship Building Strategy

docs.title: Ship Building Strategy
docs.text:
Ship building advice changes with available ships, corporation shipyards, market supply, and balance patches. Treat this page as community guidance, not game rules.

For stable mechanics, start with Ships, Ship Reference, Ship Crafting, Equip Points, and Equipment Slots.

docs.title: Choosing A First Crafted Ship
docs.text:
Good first ships depend on what your corporation can build and what role you need. Before committing resources, check:

  • Required player level and corporation membership time.
  • Skull and Relic cost.
  • Shipyard faction and level.
  • Base Equip Points and slot layout.
  • Whether the ship fills a farming, Arena, support, or PvP role.

Use Ship Reference for requirements and Faction Ship Notes for community faction notes.

docs.title: Building Around Gear
docs.text:
Most builds are constrained by Equip Points, item rank, and slot behavior.

  • Weapons, armor, shields, and storage can change Equip Point pressure through rank.
  • Weapons, drones, and batteries use first-slot versus auxiliary-slot rules.
  • Technology from auxiliary items applies in full, but item stats are reduced.
  • Diminishing returns make heavy stat stacking less efficient.

For item rules, use Item Reference, Item Upgrades, and Diminishing Returns.

docs.title: Role First
docs.text:
Pick the role before the hull:

Role Common priorities Useful references Farming Survivability, harvesting, cargo/storage, loot sources. Open World Farming Strategy, Pity Loot Arena Gear Score, legal queue setup, target pressure, survival. Arena, Arena Quick Start Fleet PvP Movement, targeting, survival, assist behavior, collision awareness. PvP Basics, Fleet PvP Strategy Carrier movement Movement pacing, survivability, Interdictor pressure. Carrier Builds, Carrier Leadership Support/repair Repair output, Technician, survivability, ally uptime. Technician, Special Items

docs.title: Structure Busting Strategy
docs.text:
Structure busting strategy changes with fleet composition, defender behavior, map layout, and balance patches. Treat this page as community guidance, not game rules.

For stable mechanics, see Structure Warfare Rules, Structures, Deployment, and Skulls.

docs.title: Practical notes
docs.text:
Community guidance from gootchat repeatedly treats structure busts as fleet operations:

  • Expect drivers, assist leaders, sort calls, and collision management.
  • Watch shield timers and Love Day / War Day timing.
  • Keep enough ships spread or rotating to avoid collision damage.
  • Defenders often use Garrisons and structure positioning to control lanes and force attackers into awkward sectors.

These are strategic patterns, not hard rules. See Fleet PvP Strategy for driver and sort-order basics.

docs.title: Strategy caveats
docs.text: Gootchat includes several tactical ideas such as low-Gear-Score-but-effective structures, lane setups, rotating smaller stacks, and using garrisons for Skull generation. Keep those labeled as community strategy unless verified as stable mechanics.

docs.text: Rule Canonical page Structure shields, Quick Jump, deployment, recall Structures and Deployment Structure Skull payout Skulls Collision limits Sectors Drivers and assist leaders Fleet PvP Rules

docs.title: The World
docs.text: The universe of The Infinite Black 2 is not a single infinite void, but a collection of distinct Solar Systems (Maps) connected by a network of Jump Gates.

docs.title: The Map Grid
docs.text: Every Solar System is a 15x15 Grid of Sectors. * Coordinates: Ranges from (0,0) in the bottom-left to (14,14) in the top-right. * Traversal: Crossing the edge of a map does not loop you around; you must use a Jump Gate to leave the system.

docs.title: Navigation
docs.text: To travel the universe, a Captain must move their fleet through individual Sectors. * Movement: Click an adjacent sector to engage engines. * Speed: Movement speed is determined by your slowest ship (or largest hull). * Safety: Some systems are patrolled by the Earth Defense Force (Safe), while others are lawless warzones (PvP). See Map Security Ratings for details.

docs.title: Topics
docs.text:
• Maps & Security Ratings - Understanding the systems.
  • Sectors & Collision - Stacking limits and how to avoid taking damage.
  • Jump Gates - How to warp between Solar Systems.
  • Factions - Wyrd, Het, Precursor, Pirates -- lore and homeworlds.

docs.title: Factions
docs.text: The Starmap is controlled by five factions. Each has its own ship variants, homeworld, and lore. Human-controlled space is safe; the other four factions control systems where PvP is possible and where corporations fight for territory and Relics.

docs.title: Faction Lore
docs.text:
Faction Summary Full article Wyrd Spider-like species; venom, mandibles, possible telepathy; transformed the Hets. Wyrd Het Once docile, now aggressive; hive-mind pods; weaponized by Wyrd; organo-metallic. Het Precursor Ancient, robotic forms; conflict with The Oblivion; cryptic guardians. Precursor Pirates Humans who fled Earth; corporate vs pirate; clones, codes of honor. Pirates Humans Corporate and EDF; Sol and colonial space; safe PvE space. --

Each faction has a homeworld at (7, 7) in its Security 9 map -- see Planets. Shipyards can specialize in one faction for ship building.

docs.title: Mechanical Differences
docs.text:
Faction affects ships, items, structures, and crafting:

Faction Stable mechanical identity Human Lower ship level requirements, cheaper advanced-ship Skull costs, safe human space, and several baseline item families. Wyrd Extra battery-slot patterns on ships, Wyrd shield/drone/item families, and Wyrd defense affinity techs around Shield Capacitors and Advanced Hacking. Pirate Pirate ship/item families, Pirate defense affinity techs around Advanced Alloys and Attack Drones, and battery bonuses that can apply broadly to Pirate ships. Het Extra drone-slot patterns on ships, Het shield/drone/item families, and Het defense affinity techs around Shield Pierce and Technician. Precursor Higher ship requirements and base EP trend, extra weapon-slot patterns, Precursor shield/drone/item families, and Precursor defense affinity techs around Shield Amplifier and Advanced Draining.

For exact ship slot, Equip Point, crafting, item, and defense rules, use the dedicated rules pages instead of repeating those tables here.

Practical faction ship advice belongs in Faction Ship Notes.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Maps
  • Structures -- Homeworlds
  • Ships -- Faction ship variants

docs.title: The Het
docs.text: The Hets are a mysterious species that live in open space and move in large herds or pods (similar to Earth's cetaceans), with an average pod size of about fifty. Individual encounters are extremely rare. Their social structure is almost nonexistent -- they seem to operate on a hive-mind, with a single unit acting only for the pod's well-being, with no regard for individuality.

docs.title: History
docs.text:
• Once docile -- Hets used to become aggressive only when threatened, preferring to flee or resorting to organized swarm attacks if necessary. They were mainly a navigation hazard, attacking ships only when their young were endangered.
  • Wyrd virus -- The Wyrd introduced a techno-organic virus to weaponize the Hets against humans. The Hets developed organo-metallic armor and bio-organic weapons.
  • Loss of control -- Contrary to Wyrd expectations, the Hets resisted control and became a major threat to both Wyrd and humans. Human technology has since adapted to harvest and integrate Het organs into their ships.

docs.title: Biology and Behavior
docs.text:
• Form: Hets resemble organic long ships with flukes and tails; they are believed to travel using solar wind currents.
  • Personality: They show no signs of distinct personalities, likely due to hive-mind dominance.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Factions
  • Wyrd | Precursor | Pirates
  • Maps
  • Ships -- Het ship variants

docs.title: The Pirate Faction
docs.text: Pirates are humans who originated on Earth in the Sol system. Severe over-pollution and resource exploitation forced many to flee; corporations saved their loyalists and abandoned the rest, leading to the death of billions. Pirate Fleets formed from survivors in ragtag, improvised ships.

docs.title: Earth and the EDF
docs.text: The Earth Defense Force is a fascist, nationalistic human faction that remains on the planet, guarding what little is left. Their main export is a constant flow of desperate colonists, the elderly, and prisoners. They often give earthlings a small ship in exchange for permanent indentured servitude.

docs.title: Humans in Space
docs.text:
• Adaptation -- Humans on other planets and stations have adapted to their environments, with visible physical and genetic differences among colonial populations. All are carbon-based, breathe oxygen, and have a nine-month gestation period; traits like hair, height, and weight vary widely.
  • Clones -- Corporations have augmented their forces with vat-grown clones engineered for war (no pain or fear). It is estimated that over 70% of all living humans in the universe are warrior clones, fighting aliens and each other.

docs.title: Corporate vs Pirate Humans
docs.text:
• Corporate -- Driven by greed; corporate espionage and deceit are common. There is no shared code of honor; profits go to leadership.
  • Pirate -- Also pursue wealth but often abandon social bonds and follow laws or a code of honor. Codes vary by clan and are important for negotiation and trade.

Humans have been in open war with every known alien species for hundreds of years. Most believe the only alternative is extinction and that survival is what matters.

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• Factions
  • Wyrd | Het | Precursor
  • Maps -- Sol and human-controlled space
  • Ships -- Pirate ship variants

docs.title: The Precursors
docs.text: The Precursors are a profoundly ancient alien race, present in the universe for billions of years. They are known for their ostensibly robotic forms -- it is unclear whether these are their real bodies or avatars. Their advanced technology includes self-destruct mechanisms that prevent capture and study, so they remain enigmatic.

docs.title: Conflict and Role
docs.text:
• The Oblivion -- The Precursors are engaged in an obscure conflict with a formidable entity known as The Oblivion. The exact nature of this struggle and its implications are not well understood.
  • Communication -- They communicate with other species, including humans, in a way that is urgent and often cryptic, suggesting they see themselves as guardians against greater cosmic threats. The extent and motivation of this protective stance are as mysterious as their origins.

docs.title: What Remains Unknown
docs.text: Little is known about their societal structures, culture, or ultimate goals. Their interactions with other species combine warning and oblique guidance, adding to their complex role in the setting.

docs.title: The Wyrd
docs.text: The Wyrd are an alien species that bear a resemblance to Earth's spiders: eight limbs -- six as legs, two with hands and multiple fingers, allowing intricate tasks with greater precision than humans. Little is known about their social structure; most knowledge comes from injured specimens.

docs.title: Biology and Behavior
docs.text:
• Size: Captured specimens are comparable in size and weight to Earth's horses.
  • Features: Eight eyes, large venomous mandibles adapted for grinding prey.
  • Venom: A potent neurotoxin that paralyzes prey while keeping it alive for feeding or storage; they may use captured prey as live hosts for their eggs.
  • Communication: Wyrd can mimic human speech; their native communication involves complex clicks and screeches. Scientists suggest a possible telepathic component -- efforts to understand their language have failed.

docs.title: Society
docs.text: All observed Wyrd have been male, leading to speculation that captured individuals are a worker or warrior caste. Other members of the species may be more dangerous, with greater size, strength, or intelligence. There is no insight into individual Wyrd personalities.

docs.title: Role in the Universe
docs.text: The Wyrd introduced a techno-organic virus to the Het in an attempt to weaponize them against humans. The Hets instead became an aggressive threat to both Wyrd and humans; humans later adapted technology to harvest and integrate Het organs into their ships.

docs.title: Ship Mechanics
docs.text: Wyrd ships are associated with shield and shield-capacitor behavior, but exact formulas still need confirmation. Practical farming and build guidance belongs in Faction Ship Notes and Open World Farming Strategy, not this faction overview.

docs.title: Maps
docs.text: The universe is made of solar systems (maps). Each map is a 15x15 grid of sectors. There are over 50 solar systems to explore, with different security levels, factions, and rewards.

docs.title: Viewing the Starmap
docs.text:
Hamburger menu (near your player button) -> Starmap.

From the Starmap you can see systems, Security Ratings, and plan travel. Use Jump Gates to move between systems.

docs.title: PvE vs PvP
docs.text:
Map type Safety Shown on Starmap Human-controlled (PvE) Safe -- no player attacks Typically blue Other factions (PvP) PvP possible Different coloring

You are always safe in human-controlled space. Beyond that, Corporations and Alliances battle for territory and Relics. Each of the five factions (Humans, Wyrd, Hets, Precursors, Pirates) controls systems and has a powerful homeworld that advanced corps can raid for high-value rewards.

docs.title: Quick Jump
docs.text:
From the hamburger menu you can Quick Jump to:

  • Deployed defense units owned by your corporation or alliance
  • Human Planets and Garrisons in human/home territory
  • Human-faction Garrisons attached to discovered Jump Gates

Cost: 1 Tech Point per jump. You cannot Quick Jump if the selected ship is already in or adjacent to the destination sector, if the destination is not deployed, or if the destination type is not eligible.

docs.title: Topics
docs.text: Topic Description Security Rating SR1-SR9: how dangerous a system is and what to expect. Sectors The 15x15 grid, movement, and collision (ship stacking limits).

docs.title: See Also
docs.text:
• The World -- Map grid and navigation
  • Jump Gates -- Moving between systems
  • PvP -- Where and why to fight

docs.title: Sectors
docs.text:
Each solar system (map) is a 15x15 grid of sectors. You move from sector to sector by clicking adjacent squares; you cannot wrap around the edge -- use a Jump Gate to leave the system.

  • Coordinates: From (0,0) at the bottom-left to (14,14) at the top-right.
  • Contents: Enemies, asteroids, other players, and structures (e.g. corp defenses, jump gates).

docs.title: Collision (Stacking Limit)
docs.text:
Too many ships in one sector causes a Collision mechanic (damage over time) to reduce lag. Collision triggers when:

Condition Limit Total ships in the sector More than 225 Ships per alliance in the sector More than 75 Enemies attacking a defense unit More than 75 (any alliance)

Turn on Display local sector ship counts in gameplay settings to see local ship counts. Observed count colors are friendly/allied ships in green, neutrals in yellow, PvP enemies in red, and NPCs in purple.

See PvP for context on alliance stacking and structure defense.

docs.title: Following Ships
docs.text: Ships can follow another ship to move together. If the followed ship gets 3 or more sectors away, the following ship immediately loses the follow and cannot refollow until it is close enough again.

docs.title: Movement timing
docs.text:
Ship size controls movement warm-up and minimum move time. See Ship and Defense Unit Size for the canonical size timing table.

Extra movement speed cannot push a ship below its size-based minimum move time, but it can still offset penalties such as enemy Interdictor Carrier effects.

docs.title: Security Rating
docs.text:
Security Rating (SR) describes how dangerous a map is. Higher-SR maps have stronger enemies, greater travel risk, and better reward potential.

Use Security Rating as a danger and reward signal, not as a complete build recommendation. Ship counts, recommended player levels, and recommended hull tiers still need live observation.

docs.title: Security Rating Table
docs.text:
Security rating Display label NPC level band Reward and rule notes SR1 Safe Zone / Security Safe Zone 2-8 Starter-safe area. SR2 Security 2 7-13 Higher NPC levels than SR1. SR3 Security 3 13-23 Ultra-Rare pity progress becomes more meaningful around this point. SR4 Security 4 23-35 Higher NPC levels and risk. SR5 Security 5 35-50 Higher NPC levels and risk. SR6 Security 6 50-80 Higher NPC levels and risk. SR7 Security 7 80-110 Higher NPC levels and risk. SR8 Security 8 110-130 25% loot boost; SR8 capture has alliance-citizen rules. SR9 Security MAX 130-150 Homeworld Raids give double pity loot.

Special encounters can use their own level bands:

Encounter type Level band Boss 140-160 Faction Boss 150-180 Instance 100-130

docs.title: What Security Rating Changes
docs.text:
• Enemies become more difficult in higher-SR maps.
  • Pity loot progresses faster in more dangerous areas.
  • Ultra-Rare pity progress is limited in lower-risk areas and becomes meaningful around SR3.
  • SR8 maps receive a 25% loot boost.
  • SR9 Homeworld Raids receive double pity loot.
  • Some alliance mechanics specifically reference SR8 map capture.

docs.title: Missing Live Data
docs.text:
These fields should be filled only from in-game observation, patch notes, or another named source.

Missing field What to collect NPC ships per sector Observed max or common range for each SR. Recommended player level Practical level range for hunting or traveling in each SR. Recommended ship/hull tier Practical minimum hull, shield, or ship class guidance. Practical danger notes Common reasons players die or avoid each SR.

If a value is a best guess, label it as Estimated instead of presenting it as a rule.
